proposals.title: Robust 3-D Path-Following for Marine Vehicles with Uncertain Thruster Dynamics: An Adaptive Observer and Control Framework
proposals.motivation: High-performance guidance and control laws, such as 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) and kinematic guidance, typically assume perfectly known actuator dynamics. In practice, thrusters are subject to degradation, faults, and hydrodynamic uncertainties, which can severely degrade path-following performance and stability guarantees. Addressing this gap is critical for the reliable long-term autonomy of AUVs and other marine vehicles in complex missions.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a model-based adaptive observer for thruster force/thrust estimation with a kinematic guidance law, it is possible to achieve robust, uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) 3-D path-following for marine vehicles despite significant uncertainty in thruster dynamics.
proposals.method: The method will extend Fossen's 3-D ALOS or virtual reference point kinematic guidance law. A nonlinear adaptive observer, based on a parameterized model of thruster dynamics (e.g., incorporating thrust coefficients and torque losses), will be designed to estimate the actual generalized forces. This estimate will feed into a backstepping or dynamic surface control loop, decoupling the kinematic guidance from the uncertain dynamics. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov theory, ensuring USGES of the combined observer-controller system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally stable, integrated guidance and control solution that is robust to thruster model errors and slow-varying faults. It will deliver a practical design framework, moving beyond the perfect actuator assumption common in guidance literature, thereby enhancing the real-world applicability of advanced guidance laws like ALOS.
proposals.novelty: While fault-tolerant control exists, the novelty lies in the tight integration of a dedicated thruster dynamics observer with a USGES-proven 3-D kinematic guidance law, explicitly relaxing a core assumption in Fossen's recent guidance work to create a robust, layered architecture.

proposals.title: A Multi-Model Adaptive Line-of-Sight (MM-ALOS) Guidance Framework for Robust Marine Vehicle Path-Following Across Maneuvering Regimes
proposals.motivation: Marine vehicles operate in diverse regimes, from high-speed, straight-line transit to low-speed, high-curvature maneuvers. A single, fixed kinematic or dynamic model, as commonly used in guidance laws like ALOS, cannot optimally capture this full range of behaviors, leading to performance trade-offs or instability. A framework that adapts to the vehicle's maneuvering state is needed for robust, high-performance guidance across all operational conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: A switched or blended multi-model guidance framework, which selects or interpolates between regime-specific ALOS formulations, will provide superior path-following performance and stability guarantees compared to a single fixed-model ALOS law across a vehicle's entire operational envelope.
proposals.method: The method will extend the 3-D ALOS guidance framework with USGES guarantees. We will define distinct kinematic models (e.g., for high-speed/low-curvature, low-speed/high-curvature, and docking regimes) and develop a switching or blending logic based on measurable states like speed, path curvature, and cross-track error. The core technical challenge is designing the switching mechanism to ensure stability during transitions, potentially leveraging concepts from switched systems theory and hysteresis to avoid chattering. The final architecture will be validated in simulation using a high-fidelity dynamic model of an AUV.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, adaptive guidance law that maintains formal stability guarantees while improving practical performance. It will provide a principled methodology for designing guidance systems that are not limited by a single model's assumptions, directly enhancing the operational versatility and safety of autonomous marine vehicles.
proposals.novelty: While multi-model adaptive control is known, its application to formally stable geometric guidance laws like ALOS is novel. The key innovation is integrating regime-dependent kinematic models into the ALOS structure while preserving its USGES properties through a carefully designed switching strategy.

proposals.title: Full-Scale Experimental Validation of a 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance and Observer-Based Control System for Large Marine Vessels
proposals.motivation: Advanced guidance and control systems for autonomous surface vessels have been validated primarily on small-scale models in controlled environments. For real-world maritime autonomy, especially for large commercial or naval vessels, the scaling effects—including complex hydrodynamics, significant inertia, actuator dynamics, and environmental disturbances—are not fully captured in small-scale tests. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in experimental proof that sophisticated algorithms like 3-D ALOS guidance and nonlinear observers perform robustly on full-scale, high-inertia platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: The 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, combined with a nonlinear passive observer for wave filtering and state estimation, will provide Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) path-following performance for a full-scale vessel, maintaining robustness against the dominant wave-frequency motions and slow maneuvering dynamics characteristic of large ships.
proposals.method: The research will implement and test a cascaded control system on a full-scale vessel (e.g., a research or supply vessel). The outer loop will use the spherical amplitude-phase 3-D ALOS guidance law to generate desired heading and velocity commands. The inner loop will use a nonlinear output-feedback controller, fed by a nonlinear passive observer for estimating low-frequency vessel pose and velocities while filtering out wave-frequency motions. The system will be validated through sea trials involving complex 3-D path-following maneuvers (e.g., docking, curved paths with depth changes for submerged appendages) in varying sea states, with performance metrics compared to high-fidelity GNSS/IMU data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental validation of a theoretically proven USGES 3-D ALOS guidance system on a full-scale marine platform, bridging a key gap between theory and real-world application. It will yield a publicly available dataset of full-scale vessel dynamics under advanced control, offering critical insights into scalability, real-world robustness, and practical implementation challenges for the marine control community.
proposals.novelty: While ALOS and observer designs are established in theory and small-scale tests, their integrated validation on a high-inertia, full-scale vessel subject to real oceanic disturbances is novel. The focus is on demonstrating stability guarantees under the pronounced scaling effects and actuator saturation inherent to large ships, which are not present in laboratory experiments.

proposals.title: Robustness Evaluation of 3-D ALOS Guidance and Nonlinear Observers for AUVs in High Sea States
proposals.motivation: Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) are increasingly deployed for complex missions, but their guidance and control systems are often validated in benign conditions. Real-world operations in high sea states (e.g., Sea State 5-6) with strong currents and wave-induced disturbances present significant challenges to stability and path-following accuracy. A systematic evaluation under these severe conditions is critical for advancing the operational envelope and reliability of marine robotic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, combined with a nonlinear passive observer for wave filtering and disturbance estimation, will maintain Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) path-following performance for an AUV model even under severe environmental disturbances, provided the observer bandwidth and guidance parameters are appropriately tuned for the disturbance spectrum.
proposals.method: The study will employ a high-fidelity 6-DOF AUV model in a simulation environment (e.g., Marine Systems Simulator) subjected to realistic wave (e.g., JONSWAP spectrum), current, and wind disturbances. The control architecture integrates the proposed spherical amplitude-phase 3-D ALOS guidance law with a nonlinear observer based on Fossen's passive observer design for wave filtering and low-frequency state estimation. Performance will be quantified using metrics like cross-track error, heading deviation, and control effort across a sweep of sea states and disturbance directions relative to the desired 3-D path.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative benchmark of the ALOS guidance and observer framework's limits in high-disturbance regimes. It will yield practical tuning guidelines for observer and guidance parameters to ensure robustness, directly contributing to the design of more reliable AUVs for offshore and scientific applications in rough seas.
proposals.novelty: While ALOS guidance and nonlinear observers are established, their integrated performance and stability guarantees are rarely tested against the full spectrum of high sea state disturbances in a 3-D path-following context. This focused robustness analysis for severe conditions fills a gap between theoretical stability proofs and practical operational validation.

proposals.title: Kinematic Path Synthesis for Marine Vehicles: A Dual Approach to Constraint-Aware Guidance
proposals.motivation: Traditional guidance and control for marine vehicles separates path planning from path following, often leading to aggressive reference paths that exceed the vehicle's dynamic capabilities, such as turn rate and acceleration limits. This necessitates complex, high-gain controllers and can compromise safety and performance. A method to inherently design paths that respect the vehicle's kinematic constraints from the outset is needed to simplify control and enhance operational reliability in complex maritime environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the path-planning problem as the kinematic dual of the path-following problem, it is possible to synthesize geometrically feasible reference paths that explicitly respect the vehicle's inherent turning rate and acceleration limits, thereby guaranteeing that a simple, low-gain guidance law like Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) can achieve stable tracking.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage a virtual target vehicle kinematics framework, where the desired path is generated as the trajectory of a virtual vehicle governed by the same kinematic constraints as the real vehicle. This ensures dynamical feasibility by construction. We will extend the 3D ALOS guidance and Virtual Reference Point concepts from Fossen's prior work to synthesize the path. The core algorithm will involve solving an inverse guidance problem: given a set of waypoints and the vehicle's maximum curvature (inverse of minimum turning radius) and torsion limits, compute a smooth, continuously differentiable path using parametric curves (e.g., Pythagorean Hodograph curves or Clothoids) whose derivatives are bounded by the kinematic constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal methodology for constraint-aware path synthesis, directly linking vehicle kinematics to reference path geometry. It will yield a computationally efficient planning tool that produces paths guaranteeing stable tracking with proven guidance laws like ALOS, reducing controller complexity and improving overall system robustness for autonomous surface and underwater vehicles.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work exists on path-following control and dynamic path planning, the explicit treatment of path design as the kinematic dual to the following problem—directly embedding the vehicle's control authority into the path's geometric formulation—represents a novel inversion of the standard guidance paradigm. This synthesizes constraint satisfaction at the planning stage rather than relying on control effort to compensate for infeasible references.

proposals.title: Disturbance Force Identification for Marine Craft: A Kinematic Observer-Based Inverse Solution for Current and Wind Estimation
proposals.motivation: Accurate real-time estimation of environmental forces (current, wind) is critical for the performance of guidance and control systems of autonomous marine vehicles. Pre-2026 methods often treat these disturbances as bounded uncertainties or require complex dynamic models with extensive parameters. A direct, model-light method for identifying these forces from standard motion data would enhance the robustness of path-following and decision-support systems in vessel traffic services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a kinematic observer, using only vehicle position, attitude, and actuator input data, can be formulated as an inverse problem to uniquely identify and decompose the dominant low-frequency environmental force vectors acting on a marine vessel, with stability guarantees akin to those in ALOS guidance.
proposals.method: The method builds upon Fossen's recent work on 3-D ALOS guidance and virtual reference point kinematics. We will design a nonlinear passive observer, but inverted: given a known guidance law and controller tracking a path, the observer will solve for the external force input that explains the discrepancy between the predicted kinematic motion (from actuator commands and nominal model) and the measured motion. The architecture will use an amplitude-phase formulation to separate current (subsurface) and wind (surface) force components, ensuring USGES-like convergence properties for the estimated disturbances.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computationally efficient 'kinematic force identification' module that can be integrated into existing guidance, navigation, and control (GNC) stacks. It will provide a formal, stable method for disturbance estimation without full dynamic model dependency, directly feeding into adaptive guidance laws and centralized traffic management decision-support systems for improved safety and energy efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the observer problem within a kinematic guidance framework specifically for force identification, rather than state estimation, and in proposing a decomposition method for environmental forces based on motion characteristics, which is not a standard feature of pre-2026 nonlinear observers for marine craft.

proposals.title: Reciprocal Intention and Trajectory Prediction for Maritime Encounter Resolution: An Observer-Centric Game-Theoretic Framework
proposals.motivation: Current maritime guidance and collision avoidance systems, like ALOS, focus on an own-ship's perspective for path-following and obstacle avoidance. For safe and efficient multi-vessel encounters, as studied in Vessel Traffic Services (VTS), it is critical to predict the intentions and trajectories of other vessels. This proposal inverts the focus: we study how a nearby observing vessel can accurately predict the subject ship's future behavior, enabling proactive and cooperative decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: An observer-centric prediction framework, combining kinematic motion models with a polymatrix game model of strategic interaction, will yield more accurate and interpretable predictions of a target vessel's intentions and short-term trajectory than purely data-driven or kinematic models alone.
proposals.method: The method first employs a kinematic guidance law model (e.g., a 3-D ALOS or virtual reference point formulation) to generate a baseline predicted trajectory for the subject ship, assuming it follows a nominal guidance objective. This prediction is then refined using a polymatrix game model, where the observer vessel infers the subject's likely strategic payoffs (e.g., COLREGs compliance, efficiency) based on relative state observations. The coupled dynamics are solved to predict the subject's most probable control actions over a receding horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, structured framework for intention recognition in marine systems, bridging the gap between single-vessel guidance and multi-vessel strategic reasoning. It will provide a theoretical foundation for next-generation decision-support systems in VTS and autonomous shipping, where understanding other agents' decision-making is paramount.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the standard guidance perspective to create an 'observer-centric' prediction model and in the unique fusion of Fossen's group's expertise in kinematic guidance laws with their recent work on polymatrix games for maritime conflict resolution.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop Adaptive ALOS Guidance Law for Biomechanics-Aware Lidar Perception and Dynamic Terrain Mapping
proposals.motivation: Studying animal locomotion in natural, unstructured terrain requires simultaneous high-fidelity mapping of the environment and estimation of the animal's internal biomechanical state. Pre-2026 sensor systems treat perception and modeling as separate, open-loop processes, leading to inefficient data collection and high uncertainty when the target or terrain moves. This limits the ability of autonomous systems to intelligently interact with dynamic biological agents in complex environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the joint problem of terrain mapping and biomechanical parameter estimation as a unified guidance and state estimation system, a Lidar's scanning pattern can be adaptively controlled to minimize the combined uncertainty of the external world model and the internal state of moving targets, yielding a superior perception-for-modeling data stream.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical control architecture. The inner loop uses a 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, treating the Lidar's focal point as a virtual vehicle to be guided. The outer loop is an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) that fuses sparse Lidar point clouds to simultaneously estimate terrain morphology and a target's kinematic chain (joint angles, velocities). The innovation is a polymatrix game-theoretic controller that arbitrates between competing guidance objectives—minimizing terrain mapping error versus biomechanical model uncertainty—to dynamically optimize the Lidar's scan pattern and frame rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel closed-loop sensor guidance framework that fuses perception with real-time model identification, 2) a provably stable (USGES) adaptive ALOS law for 3-D perception path-following, and 3) experimental validation showing improved estimation accuracy for animal gait parameters in cluttered environments compared to static sensor modes.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in treating an active sensor's scanning pattern as a kinematic guidance problem directly driven by the uncertainty of a dual (external terrain and internal biomechanics) estimation framework, a departure from traditional static or pre-programmed sensing schedules.

proposals.title: A Polymatrix Game-Based Mission Planner for AUVs in Shared Maritime Environments with 3-D ALOS Path-Following
proposals.motivation: The operational paradigm for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) is shifting from isolated missions to deployments in shared environments with manned and unmanned surface/underwater vessels. Pre-2026, collision avoidance and mission planning for AUVs often treat other vehicles as dynamic obstacles, lacking a cooperative decision-making framework that accounts for the strategic intent of all actors, which is crucial for safety and efficiency in congested or coordinated operations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a polymatrix game-theoretic decision layer with a robust 3-D guidance and control system will enable an AUV to perform complex mission planning that proactively negotiates interactions with other vessels, rather than merely reacting to them, thereby improving mission success and safety metrics.
proposals.method: The proposed architecture has two layers. A high-level mission planner formulates vessel interactions as a polymatrix game, extending Fossen's prior VTS work to a decentralized, vehicle-centric context, where the AUV's strategic decisions (e.g., lane choice, rendezvous timing) are computed based on inferred or communicated intents of other vessels. The resulting desired path and speed are fed to a low-level guidance and control system using the 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law with USGES guarantees and a virtual reference point kinematic law for precise, stable path-following of the negotiated trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, integrated decision-and-control framework that moves AUV autonomy beyond single-vehicle path-following. It will provide a formal method for AUVs to participate in implicit or explicit coordination within a multi-agent maritime system, demonstrated through simulation case studies involving crossing, overtaking, and collaborative survey scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of a strategic, game-theoretic interaction model—previously applied at a centralized VTS level—with the proven stability of 3-D ALOS guidance for an individual AUV, creating a cohesive autonomous system capable of interactive mission planning.

proposals.title: Integrated Model Predictive Control for Low-Level Motion of Marine Vehicles with High-Level ALOS Guidance
proposals.motivation: Advanced guidance laws like 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) provide excellent path-following stability but typically command desired velocities or attitudes to a separate, often simple, low-level controller. This separation can limit performance under significant environmental disturbances, actuator constraints, and dynamic coupling. A robust, unified control framework is needed to execute complex guidance commands in real-time while explicitly handling constraints and vehicle dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Model Predictive Control (MPC) scheme, designed with a computationally efficient vehicle model, can serve as a high-performance low-level motion controller that optimally tracks commands from a high-level ALOS guidance law, ensuring robust trajectory correction and constraint satisfaction in real-time.
proposals.method: The method integrates a kinematic ALOS guidance law, generating desired velocity and attitude references, with a low-level MPC. The MPC will be formulated using a discretized, simplified nonlinear dynamic model of the vehicle (e.g., a 3-DOF or 6-DOF model linearized around the reference). The optimization problem will explicitly incorporate actuator limits (saturation, rate limits) and state constraints. A real-time iteration scheme or an explicit MPC solution will be investigated for computational feasibility, with implementation tested in a high-fidelity simulation environment (e.g., using Fossen's marine vehicle models) before potential hardware-in-the-loop trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical control architecture that merges the stability guarantees of ALOS guidance with the constraint-handling and optimal performance of MPC for marine vehicles. It will provide a rigorous framework for real-time, low-level motion control that is robust to disturbances and model uncertainties, advancing beyond standard PID or backstepping inner-loop designs.
proposals.novelty: While MPC is established in process engineering, its tight integration with provably stable geometric guidance laws like ALOS for marine systems is novel. The key innovation is the structured synthesis of a kinematic guidance layer with a dynamic MPC layer, creating a cohesive system with verifiable end-to-end performance under constraints.

proposals.title: Reactive Mission and Motion Planning for Large Marine Vessels: A Kinematic Guidance and Polymatrix Game Framework for Autonomous Surface Ships
proposals.motivation: The push for autonomous surface ships (AShS) and offshore installations is accelerating, driven by safety, efficiency, and operational cost goals. However, scaling reactive planning frameworks from small, agile AUVs to large, inertia-dominated vessels like cargo ships or oil rigs presents a critical challenge. These vessels have vastly different dynamics, limited maneuverability, and must operate in complex, rule-constrained environments like harbors and offshore fields, where traditional path-following alone is insufficient for collision avoidance and mission adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical reactive planning framework, combining a high-level polymatrix game for cooperative conflict resolution with a low-level 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) kinematic guidance law, can be effectively scaled to guarantee safe and stable mission execution for large marine vessels under environmental disturbances and dynamic obstacles.
proposals.method: The framework will be structured in two layers. The high-level mission planner will implement a polymatrix game formulation, as used in vessel traffic services, to resolve multi-agent conflicts and generate dynamically feasible, COLREG-compliant waypoints for the own vessel. The low-level motion controller will employ a 3-D ALOS guidance law, extended from Fossen's recent work, to generate velocity commands for the vessel's autopilot. This law will be adapted for surface vessels using a virtual reference point and a spherical amplitude-phase formulation to handle the significant inertia and underactuation, ensuring uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path-following of the reactive waypoint sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated framework for reactive planning specifically tailored to the dynamics and operational constraints of large autonomous marine vessels. It will contribute a concrete methodology for bridging strategic conflict resolution with stable, low-level kinematic guidance, a gap in current AShS research. The result will be a step towards certifiable autonomy for complex maritime operations.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a strategic polymatrix game-theoretic decision layer with a proven, stability-guaranteed 3-D ALOS kinematic guidance law, specifically configured for the scale and inertia of ships and offshore platforms, moves beyond applications for small, agile vehicles.

proposals.title: Optimal Sensor Placement for Fixed-Path AUVs: A Kinematic Guidance and Polymatrix Game Theoretic Approach
proposals.motivation: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) missions for seabed mapping or environmental monitoring are constrained by energy and time. While extensive pre-2026 research focuses on optimal path planning for given sensor locations, the inverse problem—optimally placing sensors or sampling points for a predetermined, feasible AUV trajectory—is under-explored. Solving this is critical for maximizing information gain (e.g., spatial coverage, target detection probability) within strict mission durations, directly impacting operational efficiency and data quality.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the sensor placement problem as a constrained resource allocation over a fixed 3-D kinematic path, a solution combining adaptive guidance principles and game-theoretic negotiation will yield a globally efficient, computationally tractable allocation that respects the vehicle's kinematic constraints and mission time limit.
proposals.method: The method leverages the researcher's expertise in 3-D guidance. First, the fixed AUV path is parameterized using a virtual reference point, as in Fossen's recent work, to define the kinematic reachable set along the trajectory. Candidate sensor locations are treated as players in a polymatrix game, where payoffs are based on information utility and cost functions related to the deviation from the nominal path required for measurement. A centralised decision-support algorithm, inspired by the cited VTS research, will solve this game to compute the optimal subset and sequence of sensor visits that maximize total mission utility without exceeding the time constraint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for mission planning that decouples path feasibility from sensor optimization, offering mission planners a tool to maximize data return from logistically constrained trajectories. It will extend game-theoretic and guidance-based methods to a new class of inverse problems in marine robotics, contributing a formal methodology for resource-constrained sensor tasking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classic guidance problem and applying a polymatrix game formulation—typically used for multi-agent collision avoidance—to the single-vehicle, multi-task sensor placement problem on a fixed path, which is a distinct and underexplored paradigm compared to simultaneous path and sensor planning.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Observer-ALOS Guidance Scheme for Robust 3-D Path-Following with Intermittent Position Feedback
proposals.motivation: For autonomous underwater and surface vehicles, reliable position measurements from GPS or acoustic systems are often lost or available only at low, irregular intervals due to signal occlusion, multipath effects, or energy-saving duty cycles. This intermittency severely degrades the performance of standard guidance laws like Line-of-Sight (LOS), which typically assume continuous state feedback, leading to potential mission failure or reduced operational safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a kinematic model-based nonlinear observer with an Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, we can achieve Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) path-following for marine vehicles even when position updates are sporadic, provided the overall system remains observable.
proposals.method: The method builds on Fossen's recent 3-D ALOS and virtual reference point guidance frameworks. A continuous-time nonlinear observer will be designed to propagate the vehicle's position and velocity states using a kinematic model, receiving intermittent GPS/position fixes as discrete measurement updates. The observer's continuously estimated states will feed the ALOS guidance law, which generates desired attitude/velocity commands. The closed-loop stability will be analyzed using hybrid systems theory (modeled after the 2012 work of Goebel, Sanfelice, and Teel) to formally guarantee USGES under specific minimum update rates and maximum data-loss durations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally stable, integrated observer-guidance solution for marine vehicles operating with intermittent positioning. It will extend the applicability of proven ALOS guidance to realistic, communication-constrained scenarios. The result will be a set of verifiable design rules relating observer gains, update rates, and guaranteed path-following performance.
proposals.novelty: While observers for navigation are common, the novel integration of a hybrid observer with a USGES-proven 3-D ALOS guidance law, analyzed formally within a hybrid systems framework for intermittent measurements, is unexplored. This moves beyond standard cascaded designs to a unified stability guarantee for the entire guidance-and-estimation loop under measurement loss.

proposals.title: A Kinematic Guidance and Control Framework for Underactuated Marine Vehicles Using a Virtual Reference Point and Adaptive Line-of-Sight
proposals.motivation: Most advanced guidance laws for 3D path-following, such as the 3D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) and Virtual Reference Point (VRP) methods, assume a fully actuated vehicle model. This is a significant limitation for real-world underactuated ships and AUVs, which lack independent control in sway and heave. Extending these high-performance guidance laws to underactuated systems is a critical step toward their practical maritime application.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a kinematic VRP guidance law with a dynamic control allocator and stability-proven ALOS principles, a unified guidance and control framework can be developed that guarantees Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) path-following for underactuated marine vehicles.
proposals.method: The method will first reformulate the 3D VRP/ALOS guidance laws to produce desired kinematic velocities for an underactuated model. A cascaded control system will be designed: the outer loop generates guidance commands, while an inner-loop dynamic controller uses feedback linearization or backstepping to track these commands, explicitly handling the non-holonomic constraints. Stability will be proven using cascaded systems theory and Lyapunov methods, ensuring the USGES property is retained despite the reduced actuation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally stable, integrated guidance and control solution for underactuated vehicles performing 3D path-following, directly extending Fossen's recent theoretical advances to a broader class of practical systems. It will deliver a verifiable control architecture and simulation benchmarks for underactuated ships and AUVs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of the VRP and spherical ALOS guidance paradigms—recently developed for fully actuated systems—with underactuated dynamic control, providing an end-to-end stability guarantee not commonly found in prior underactuated path-following work.

proposals.title: A Wave-Filtering Observer for Kinematic Guidance: Integrating Explicit Wave Motion Estimation into 3-D ALOS Path-Following
proposals.motivation: For autonomous surface vessels (ASVs) and underwater vehicles operating near the surface, wave-induced motions degrade the performance of guidance and control systems. Pre-2026 standard practice often treats wave disturbances as noise to be filtered out by the controller, but this separation can lead to suboptimal path-following and increased actuator wear. Explicitly estimating wave-frequency components within the observer structure is a critical step toward higher precision in marine operations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a wave-frequency model directly into the kinematic observer design will yield more accurate estimates of the vessel's low-frequency motion, leading to improved stability and performance of 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance laws in a seaway.
proposals.method: The method extends the kinematic guidance observer framework from Fossen's recent work by augmenting the state vector with states representing wave-induced motions, modeled as outputs of a linear oscillator driven by white noise (a simplified model of the wave spectrum). A nonlinear passive observer (NPO) or Kalman filter structure will be designed to simultaneously estimate the vessel's low-frequency position/attitude and the wave-frequency components. This estimated wave motion will then be compensated for in the virtual reference point calculation of the 3-D ALOS guidance law, ensuring the guidance commands are based on the intended low-frequency vessel motion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified observer-guidance architecture that explicitly accounts for wave dynamics, advancing beyond ad-hoc filtering. It will deliver a rigorous stability analysis (USGES/UES) for the coupled observer-guidance system and demonstrate, through simulation with standard wave spectra, reduced cross-track error and smoother control actions compared to baseline designs.
proposals.novelty: While wave filtering for control is well-studied, its tight integration into a *kinematic* guidance observer for 3-D ALOS path-following is novel. This approach moves the wave compensation upstream into the guidance loop, rather than relying solely on the control system to reject disturbances.

proposals.title: Integral Barrier Lyapunov Function-Based Nonlinear Control for Marine Vehicles with Actuator Dynamics and Saturation
proposals.motivation: High-performance guidance and control for autonomous marine vehicles, such as AUVs and USVs, requires robust handling of actuator limitations. Pre-2026 control designs, including Fossen's ALOS and virtual reference point methods, often assume ideal thrusters, leading to performance degradation or instability when real-world thruster dynamics and saturation constraints are encountered. Addressing this gap is critical for safe and reliable autonomous operations in complex maritime environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly incorporating a dynamic thruster model and saturation limits into the control Lyapunov function design, a nonlinear controller can be synthesized that guarantees uniform semiglobal exponential stability (USGES) for the closed-loop path-following system, even under aggressive maneuvers and saturated control inputs.
proposals.method: The method will extend the 3-D ALOS guidance law with a cascaded control architecture. The outer loop will use the virtual reference point kinematic guidance. The inner loop will synthesize a nonlinear dynamic controller using an Integral Barrier Lyapunov Function (IBLF) framework. The IBLF will directly encode the thruster saturation limits and a first-order actuator dynamic model, transforming the constrained input problem into an unconstrained state constraint problem. Backstepping and Lyapunov redesign techniques will be employed to ensure stability guarantees for the complete vehicle-actuator system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a provably stable, unified control design framework for marine vehicles that rigorously accounts for actuator dynamics and saturation. It will deliver a directly implementable control law, enhancing the practical applicability of advanced guidance laws like ALOS. The results will be validated in high-fidelity simulations with a dynamic ship/AUV model, demonstrating improved performance under saturation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of the IBLF technique with the ALOS and virtual reference point guidance paradigm specifically for marine vehicles, offering a formal stability guarantee (USGES) for the combined guidance-control-actuator system under saturation—a step beyond ad-hoc anti-windup or robust control methods common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Observer-Backstepping Guidance and Control for Micro-AUVs: A Resource-Constrained Implementation with Stability Guarantees
proposals.motivation: The deployment of low-cost, micro-scale Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (µAUVs) for environmental monitoring and confined-space inspection is a growing pre-2026 research area. However, their severe constraints in sensor quality, computational power, and actuator precision render advanced guidance and control laws, like observer-backstepping, non-trivial to implement. There is a critical gap in adapting these provably stable methods for platforms where standard sensing and high-fidelity dynamic models are unavailable.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified, kinematic-based observer-backstepping control architecture, leveraging a virtual reference point and adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance, can be implemented on µAUVs to achieve globally stable path-following, even with significant sensor noise and model uncertainty.
proposals.method: The method adapts Fossen's Virtual Reference Point (VRP) kinematic guidance law and 3-D ALOS framework for µAUVs. A reduced-order, kinematic observer will be designed to estimate the required states (e.g., sideslip) from low-cost, noisy inertial and Doppler velocity log measurements. This will be integrated with a simplified backstepping controller that accounts for dominant, low-frequency dynamics only. The stability analysis will focus on Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) guarantees under the new practical constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully specified, resource-aware guidance and control algorithm for µAUVs with formal stability proofs. It will provide a benchmark for implementing advanced nonlinear control on severely constrained platforms and validate the approach through high-fidelity simulation with realistic sensor and actuator models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic co-design of a state observer and backstepping controller explicitly for the kinematic and computational limitations of µAUVs, bridging the gap between high-performance theoretical control and low-cost robotic implementation.

proposals.title: Nonlinear Output Feedback Control for Dynamic Positioning of Floating Offshore Platforms Using a Virtual Reference Point Kinematic Guidance Law
proposals.motivation: Dynamic positioning (DP) of floating offshore platforms, such as semi-submersibles and FPSOs, is critical for safe and efficient offshore operations, including drilling, production, and wind turbine maintenance. Existing DP systems often rely on linearized models or PID controllers, which can struggle with the significant nonlinearities, low-frequency environmental disturbances, and actuator constraints inherent to these large floating structures. A robust, nonlinear control solution is needed to improve station-keeping performance and reduce fuel consumption.
proposals.hypothesis: A nonlinear output feedback control architecture, integrating a 3-D Virtual Reference Point (VRP) kinematic guidance law with an observer-based backstepping controller, will provide Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) station-keeping for floating offshore platforms under environmental disturbances, outperforming conventional DP controllers in terms of robustness and precision.
proposals.method: The method will extend the 3-D VRP guidance law, previously developed for AUV path-following, to generate a virtual target point for the platform's desired position and heading. This will be integrated with a nonlinear output feedback controller using a nonlinear passive observer (NPO) to estimate low-frequency vessel motions and wave-frequency disturbances from position and heading measurements. A backstepping control law will be designed to force the platform's kinematics to track the VRP, ensuring USGES stability guarantees. The complete system will be validated in simulations using a nonlinear 3-DOF (surge, sway, yaw) model of a semi-submersible platform subject to wind, current, and wave forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically rigorous nonlinear control framework for DP of large floating platforms, explicitly guaranteeing USGES stability. It will demonstrate the successful transfer of guidance-based control paradigms from marine vehicles to offshore structures. The results will offer a practical control design with improved disturbance rejection for the offshore industry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the application of a VRP-based kinematic guidance law—a concept from autonomous vehicle path-following—to the station-keeping problem of floating platforms, and its integration into a full nonlinear output feedback DP architecture with formal stability proofs, which is not common in standard industrial DP systems.

proposals.title: Adaptive 3-D Line-of-Sight Guidance for Marine Vehicles with Time-Varying Sideslip Compensation
proposals.motivation: In marine guidance and control, the standard Line-of-Sight (LOS) algorithm and its 3-D extensions often assume a constant or zero sideslip angle, representing a steady current or wind. This assumption is frequently violated in dynamic ocean environments, leading to path-following errors and degraded performance. Addressing time-varying sideslip is crucial for the robust autonomy of underwater and surface vehicles operating in realistic, variable conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating an adaptive observer to estimate a time-varying sideslip angle directly into a 3-D ALOS guidance law, we can achieve uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path-following for marine vehicles under dynamically changing ocean currents and wind disturbances.
proposals.method: We will extend the spherical amplitude-phase 3-D ALOS guidance framework, which provides USGES guarantees, by relaxing the constant sideslip assumption. A nonlinear adaptive observer, based on vehicle kinematics and available velocity/heading measurements, will be designed to estimate the time-varying sideslip angle online. This estimate will be fed back into the guidance law's virtual target dynamics and lookahead calculations. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov theory, combining the USGES properties of the baseline ALOS with the convergence properties of the adaptive estimator.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously stable 3-D path-following guidance law that is robust to time-varying environmental disturbances, a significant step beyond constant-parameter models. It will deliver a practical algorithm for AUVs and USVs, enhancing operational reliability in complex coastal and oceanographic missions. The integration of adaptive estimation within the proven ALOS structure offers a theoretically sound and applicable solution.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive LOS for constant sideslip exists, the explicit integration of a time-varying sideslip estimator within a 3-D ALOS framework with formal USGES stability guarantees is novel. This moves beyond treating disturbance as a constant parameter bias to actively compensating for its dynamic evolution.

proposals.title: Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance with Integrated Hydrodynamic Parameter Estimation for Underactuated Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Advanced 3-D guidance laws, such as Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS), provide robust path-following for underactuated vehicles by adapting to kinematic uncertainties. However, their performance can degrade when coupled with a standard autopilot due to unmodeled vehicle dynamics, particularly unknown or varying hydrodynamic parameters like damping and added mass. Compensating for this uncertainty is critical for the precise, energy-efficient operation of autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) in complex missions.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a direct adaptive control law for hydrodynamic parameter estimation within the ALOS guidance framework, the overall path-following system can achieve uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) convergence despite significant dynamic model uncertainty, outperforming cascaded guidance-and-control designs.
proposals.method: The method extends the spherical amplitude-phase ALOS formulation with a virtual reference point. A Lyapunov-based adaptive law will be designed to estimate key hydrodynamic parameters (e.g., linear and quadratic damping coefficients) online. This estimator will be tightly coupled with the guidance law, feeding forward compensation terms to the inner-loop controller (e.g., a backstepping or sliding-mode controller) to account for dynamic forces. Stability will be proven using cascaded systems theory and Lyapunov analysis, ensuring USGES guarantees for the complete closed-loop system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified guidance-and-control design that explicitly compensates for dynamic uncertainty within the guidance loop, enhancing robustness for underactuated AUVs. It will provide a rigorous stability proof for the integrated adaptive system and demonstrate performance improvements in simulations with high-fidelity vehicle models.
proposals.novelty: While ALOS handles kinematic unknowns and separate adaptive control exists for dynamics, the novelty lies in the direct integration of hydrodynamic parameter adaptation into the ALOS guidance law's stability framework, creating a cohesive adaptive system rather than a cascaded, separately tuned architecture.

proposals.title: Robust 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance with Stochastic Stability Guarantees under Measurement Noise and Delays
proposals.motivation: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) rely on guidance laws like Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) for precise 3-D path-following. However, in real-world operations, these systems are degraded by noisy sensor measurements (e.g., from DVL, AHRS) and communication-induced state estimation delays, which can compromise stability and tracking performance. Existing ALOS formulations with Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) guarantees typically assume perfect, instantaneous state feedback, limiting their practical applicability.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a stochastic disturbance model and a predictor-based observer into the spherical amplitude-phase ALOS framework, we can derive a robust guidance law that maintains stochastic stability and prescribed performance bounds in the presence of bounded measurement noise and known, constant time delays.
proposals.method: The method extends the 3-D ALOS kinematic guidance law using a spherical coordinate formulation. We will model position and heading measurement noise as bounded stochastic disturbances. A model-based predictor, such as a Smith predictor or a finite-spectrum assignment technique, will be designed to compensate for known communication delays in the state feedback loop. The combined observer-guidance structure's stability will be analyzed using stochastic Lyapunov-Krasovskii functionals, leading to explicit bounds on path-following error variance. Validation will be conducted via high-fidelity numerical simulations of an AUV model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally robust 3-D ALOS guidance law with verifiable stochastic stability guarantees, directly addressing a key implementation gap. It will deliver a practical design framework for AUV operators to tune guidance performance against known sensor and network characteristics, enhancing the reliability of autonomous marine missions.
proposals.novelty: While delay compensation and noise rejection are studied separately in control, their integrated treatment within the specific spherical amplitude-phase ALOS structure to provide stochastic stability guarantees is novel. This moves beyond deterministic USGES results to a more practical robustness analysis for marine guidance systems.

proposals.title: A Time-Adaptive Line-of-Sight (T-ALOS) Guidance Law for Coordinated Path Following of Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Current 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance provides robust path-following but lacks explicit temporal control, which is critical for coordinated multi-vehicle operations and mission scenarios requiring arrival at waypoints at precise times. In pre-2026 maritime and underwater robotics, this gap limits the ability to execute synchronized surveys, dynamic rendezvous, or cooperative tasks under environmental disturbances.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a time-dependent virtual target evolution law with the proven stability properties of ALOS guidance, we can achieve uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path following with guaranteed temporal convergence to specified spatiotemporal targets.
proposals.method: We will extend the spherical amplitude-phase ALOS formulation by defining a time-parameterized desired path. A time-to-go error state will be incorporated into the virtual target update law, dynamically adjusting the along-path speed based on a temporal Lyapunov function. The control architecture will fuse this temporal guidance law with a backstepping controller for an underactuated AUV model, ensuring stability proofs via cascaded systems theory and ISS analysis for disturbance rejection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, stable guidance framework for time-constrained path following, directly applicable to coordinated AUV missions. It will extend the ALOS theoretical foundation to explicitly handle temporal specifications, a key step towards more autonomous and synchronized marine vehicle systems.
proposals.novelty: While existing ALOS handles spatial convergence and kinematic adaptation, the explicit, provably stable integration of a time-constraint mechanism within the USGES ALOS framework is novel. This moves beyond pure path following into the domain of trajectory tracking with an adaptive, guidance-oriented approach.

proposals.title: A Unified Guidance System for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles: Integrating 3-D ALOS Path-Following with Reactive Obstacle Avoidance
proposals.motivation: For autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) to operate reliably in complex, cluttered environments, they must execute precise path-following while reacting to unforeseen obstacles. Pre-2026 guidance systems, like the 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) law, offer provable stability for path-following but are typically designed without integrated reactive obstacle avoidance. This gap limits safe autonomous navigation in dynamic real-world scenarios such as underwater infrastructure inspection or seabed surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified guidance law can be synthesized by blending the output of a provably stable 3-D ALOS path-following controller with a reactive, velocity obstacle-based avoidance module, ensuring globally uniformly ultimately bounded (GUUB) stability of the combined system and collision-free navigation.
proposals.method: The method builds upon Fossen's recent 3-D ALOS guidance with a virtual reference point. A reactive obstacle avoidance module, based on the Velocity Obstacle (VO) or Dynamic Window Approach (DWA), will generate an avoidance velocity command. A novel, smooth blending function, using a potential field or a nonlinear modulation of the ALOS guidance vector, will integrate the avoidance command with the ALOS-derived desired velocity. The combined guidance law will be analyzed using Lyapunov methods and Input-to-State Stability (ISS) to guarantee stability, and validated in simulations with a 6-DOF AUV model encountering static and dynamic obstacles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formally analyzed, unified guidance architecture for AUVs that guarantees both path-following stability and safe obstacle avoidance. It will provide a practical framework for deploying stable guidance laws in cluttered environments, advancing the field of marine cybernetics. The results will be demonstrated through high-fidelity simulations, providing a benchmark for integrated guidance and control systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal stability-guaranteed integration of a provably stable 3-D ALOS guidance law, designed for precise geometric path-following, with a reactive, real-time obstacle avoidance system, moving beyond sequential or switching architectures common in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Model-Free Online Sideslip Estimation for Marine Vehicles via Extremum Seeking Control
proposals.motivation: Accurate sideslip angle estimation is critical for high-precision guidance and path-following of marine vehicles, as it compensates for drift caused by currents and wind. Traditional model-based observers require accurate hydrodynamic parameters, which are often uncertain or time-varying. A model-free, online estimation method would enhance the robustness of guidance laws, particularly the 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) methods central to Fossen's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: The sideslip angle can be accurately estimated in real-time by applying extremum seeking control (ESC) to minimize a suitable cost function derived from cross-track error, without requiring a detailed dynamic model of the vehicle.
proposals.method: The method integrates a standard kinematic guidance law (e.g., ALOS) with a perturbation-based ESC loop. The cost function for the ESC will be the squared cross-track error relative to a desired path. The ESC algorithm will perturb an initial estimate of the sideslip angle, measure the resulting gradient in the cost, and adapt the estimate to drive the error to a minimum. Stability analysis will employ averaging and singular perturbation theories to guarantee uniform semi-global exponential practical stability (USGES) for the combined guidance-and-estimation system.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, model-free sideslip estimator that can be seamlessly integrated with existing kinematic guidance laws. It will provide a rigorous stability guarantee for the combined system, enhancing the operational robustness of autonomous underwater and surface vehicles in uncertain flow environments. The result is a practical tool that moves parameter adaptation from the vehicle dynamics into the guidance loop.
proposals.novelty: While ESC is established for parameter tuning in control systems, its direct application to online sideslip estimation as a model-free augmentation to kinematic guidance laws is novel. This approach bypasses the need for a dynamic model or explicit current measurement, differing fundamentally from conventional model-based observers or Kalman filters used in marine vehicle navigation.

proposals.title: MPC-based 3-D Path Following for Marine Vehicles with Explicit Curvature Preview and Input Constraints
proposals.motivation: Advanced guidance laws like 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) and virtual reference point kinematics provide robust stability but are fundamentally reactive. For complex, high-curvature paths, performance degrades due to a lack of preview. Model Predictive Control (MPC) is a natural framework to incorporate path preview and actuator constraints, but its application to the specific 3-D path-following problem for underactuated marine vehicles remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a kinematic guidance law within an MPC formulation that explicitly previews future path curvature will yield superior tracking performance and smoother control actions for underactuated AUVs and USVs, compared to standard ALOS, especially on paths with varying curvature.
proposals.method: The method will formulate the path-following error dynamics using a Serret-Frenet frame aligned with a virtual target, extending Fossen's kinematic guidance laws. A discrete-time linear time-varying (LTV) model will be derived by linearizing these dynamics around the previewed path. A quadratic MPC cost function will penalize cross-track, along-track, and vertical errors, while explicitly incorporating constraints on actuator rates and magnitudes. The optimization will be solved in receding horizon using established pre-2026 QP solvers, with the virtual target's motion governed by the MPC solution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous MPC framework for 3-D marine vehicle path-following that guarantees constraint satisfaction. It will deliver a direct performance comparison between predictive (MPC) and reactive (ALOS) guidance in simulation and potentially field trials, quantifying benefits in terms of tracking accuracy and control effort on benchmark paths.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of Fossen's well-established 3-D guidance kinematics with a predictive control scheme that explicitly uses previewed geometric path data (curvature, torsion) as a time-varying parameter in the MPC model, moving beyond standard setpoint tracking MPC formulations.

proposals.title: Integral Line-of-Sight Guidance with Nonlinear Disturbance Observer for Sideslip Compensation in Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Accurate path-following for marine vehicles is critically degraded by environmental disturbances like currents and waves, which induce sideslip. Pre-2026 guidance laws, such as Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS), often treat sideslip as a constant or slowly varying parameter, limiting performance in dynamic, high-disturbance environments. Robust, real-time estimation and compensation of time-varying sideslip is essential for the operational reliability of autonomous surface and underwater vehicles.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDOB) into a 3-D integral Line-of-Sight (ILOS) guidance structure will provide accurate, real-time estimation of time-varying sideslip, enabling a guidance law with uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path-following error dynamics in the presence of persistent environmental disturbances.
proposals.method: The method builds upon Fossen's 3-D ALOS and virtual reference point kinematic guidance frameworks. A first-order or higher-order NDOB will be designed to estimate the time-varying sideslip angle from cross-track and vertical-track error dynamics. This estimate will be fed into an integral LOS guidance law, where the observer's output directly compensates the lookahead-based steering laws. The closed-loop stability of the combined observer-guidance-controller system will be rigorously analyzed using Lyapunov theory and cascade arguments to guarantee USGES.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, provably stable guidance-observer architecture that explicitly handles time-varying sideslip, enhancing the robustness of marine vehicle path-following. It will extend the theoretical foundation of ALOS guidance by formally integrating disturbance observation, providing a practical design tool validated through numerical simulation with high-fidelity vehicle models and realistic disturbance profiles.
proposals.novelty: While NDOBs have been used in vehicle control loops, their tight integration into the kinematic guidance law for direct sideslip compensation within Fossen's USGES ALOS framework is novel. This moves beyond parameter adaptation to dynamic disturbance estimation, offering a more responsive solution for time-varying disturbances compared to existing integral or adaptive LOS methods.

proposals.title: Distributed 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance for Cooperative Path-Following in Micro-AUV Swarms
proposals.motivation: Micro-AUV swarms offer transformative potential for high-resolution environmental monitoring and confined-space inspection. However, scaling down classical guidance and control methods presents significant challenges due to severe computational, communication, and sensing constraints inherent to micro-scale vehicles. Existing swarm coordination strategies often lack the rigorous stability guarantees and adaptability to unknown currents required for robust 3-D underwater operations.
proposals.hypothesis: The 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, proven for single vehicles, can be effectively distributed and scaled to coordinate a swarm of micro-AUVs, achieving globally stable cooperative path-following even under significant inter-vehicle and environmental disturbances.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed, hierarchical control architecture. A virtual reference point kinematic guidance law will generate a desired velocity field for the swarm's geometric formation. Each micro-AUV will then execute a local 3-D ALOS law, adapting its lookahead distance based on local current estimates to achieve USGES path-following. The coordination layer will use a consensus-based protocol, inspired by polymatrix game concepts for distributed decision-making, to synchronize the virtual reference points along the desired 3-D path, ensuring cohesive swarm motion.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, stability-guaranteed guidance framework specifically designed for resource-constrained micro-AUVs. It will extend the theoretical foundation of ALOS to distributed multi-agent systems and provide a practical control solution for 3-D cooperative missions, validated through high-fidelity simulations of a micro-swarm in turbulent flow fields.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a rigorously stable 3-D ALOS core with a lightweight, game-inspired distributed coordination protocol, tailored explicitly for the severe operational constraints of micro-underwater vehicles, represents a significant advance over centralized or less formally guaranteed swarm guidance methods.

proposals.title: Robust 3-D Path-Following for Large, Underactuated Ships: A Kinematic Guidance and Control Design with Stability Guarantees
proposals.motivation: The autonomous operation of large marine vessels, such as tankers and bulk carriers, presents a significant challenge due to their underactuated nature, slow dynamics, and high inertia. Existing guidance and control laws, often validated on smaller, more agile vehicles, may not guarantee the required robustness and stability for safe, precise path-following in complex 3-D environments (e.g., port approaches with depth variations). Addressing this gap is crucial for advancing maritime autonomy and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: The 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law and Virtual Reference Point (VRP) kinematic framework, proven for smaller AUVs, can be systematically scaled and integrated with a ship-specific controller to achieve Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) path-following for large, underactuated ships, despite their slow response and environmental disturbances.
proposals.method: The research will extend the spherical amplitude-phase ALOS formulation and VRP kinematic guidance law to the 3-DOF (surge, sway, yaw) and 4-DOF (including roll) models of large ships. A cascaded control system will be designed, where the outer loop generates guidance commands using the adapted ALOS/VRP laws, and an inner-loop controller (e.g., a backstepping or sliding-mode design) tracks these commands. Stability will be analyzed using cascaded systems theory and Lyapunov methods to formally prove USGES. High-fidelity simulations using models of a large tanker will validate performance under wind, current, and wave disturbances.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously stable guidance and control architecture specifically tailored for large, underactuated ships, filling a critical gap between theory and practical application. It will provide a benchmark framework for scaling advanced guidance laws from smaller vehicles to capital ships, contributing directly to the field of marine cyber-physical systems and autonomous shipping.
proposals.novelty: While ALOS and VRP methods exist, their application and stability-guaranteed integration into a full control stack for high-inertia, underactuated surface ships is novel. The explicit consideration of 3-D path-following (including depth/roll) for such vessels, with formal stability proofs, goes beyond typical 2-D surface ship autopilot designs.

proposals.title: Inverse Path Planning for Environmental Drift Estimation: A Kinematic Observer-Based Approach Using Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance
proposals.motivation: Autonomous marine and underwater vehicles operate in environments with significant, often unknown, drift forces from currents and waves. Existing guidance and control systems compensate for these disturbances reactively, but a method to explicitly infer the environmental forces from observed vehicle behavior would enable improved path planning, adaptive control, and environmental mapping. This inverse problem is critical for enhancing the autonomy and safety of marine operations.
proposals.hypothesis: The environmental drift forces acting on a vehicle can be accurately estimated by inverting a known, stable guidance and control law, using the discrepancy between the vehicle's commanded kinematic guidance and its actual trajectory as an observation signal.
proposals.method: The method will leverage the team's expertise in 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance and virtual reference point kinematics. We will formulate an inverse kinematic observer that treats the known guidance law (e.g., ALOS) and low-level controller as a forward model. By comparing the vehicle's actual trajectory (from navigation sensors) with the trajectory predicted by the forward model in a disturbance-free environment, we will construct a kinematic estimation error. A Lyapunov-based adaptive observer will be designed to estimate the unknown drift velocity, guaranteeing Uniform Semiglobal Exponential Stability (USGES) for the error dynamics, consistent with pre-2026 stability guarantees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for online environmental force estimation without requiring direct force measurements or complex dynamic models. It will contribute a theoretically sound, kinematic-based observer that integrates seamlessly with existing guidance systems, advancing the field of marine vehicle autonomy towards greater situational awareness and adaptive capability.
proposals.novelty: While disturbance observers exist in control theory, this approach uniquely inverts a kinematic guidance law for environmental mapping, framing it as an inverse path-planning problem rather than a dynamic control problem. The use of a stable ALOS guidance framework as the forward model for force inference is a novel synthesis of Fossen's guidance themes.

proposals.title: Energy-Optimal Path Generation for Marine Vehicles with Persistent Sideslip: A Kinematic Guidance and Polymatrix Game Framework
proposals.motivation: Autonomous surface and underwater vehicles often operate under persistent sideslip due to currents, wind, or wave drift, forcing guidance and control systems to expend significant energy on compensation. Pre-2026 guidance laws, like 3D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS), correct for drift but do not co-design the nominal path for optimality under this known disturbance. Generating paths that are inherently robust to a known drift profile is crucial for extending mission endurance in oceanographic surveys and offshore operations.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating path generation as an optimal control problem that explicitly incorporates a known, constant sideslip angle into the vehicle kinematics, the resulting nominal paths will require less corrective control effort and lower total energy consumption for path-following compared to standard geometric paths followed by drift-compensating guidance laws.
proposals.method: The method integrates Fossen's recent work on kinematic guidance and game theory. First, we derive a kinematic model where the sideslip angle is a fixed parameter. We then pose an energy-optimal path generation problem, solved via direct collocation or Pontryagin's principle, to produce nominal 3D paths. The path-following controller will use a modified 3D ALOS law. A polymatrix game framework, inspired by recent VTS research, will be adapted to model the interaction between path geometry and environmental forces, informing robust path selection in multi-vehicle scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a systematic methodology for generating energy-optimal reference paths for vehicles with known drift, reducing the burden on the tracking controller. It will extend kinematic guidance theory by coupling path planning with drift robustness at a fundamental level. The adaptation of the polymatrix game to this context offers a novel decision-support tool for fleet energy management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard approach: instead of designing a guidance law to compensate for drift on a pre-defined path, we design the path itself to be optimal and inherently compatible with the drift. Combining this optimal kinematic design with a game-theoretic multi-agent framework for path selection is also novel.

proposals.title: Adaptive Kinematic Guidance for Robust Legged Locomotion and Landing via Proprioceptive-Visual Fusion
proposals.motivation: Autonomous legged robots and drones operating in natural, GPS-denied environments (e.g., disaster zones, planetary exploration) require robust navigation on unknown, unprepared terrain. Current methods rely heavily on high-fidelity exteroceptive sensors (LiDAR, stereo cameras) which are fragile, computationally expensive, and fail under poor visibility. A method using only noisy, monocular vision and proprioception would enable resilient autonomy with low-cost, commodity hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating terrain geometry and visual uncertainty as a unified adaptive inference problem within a kinematic guidance framework, a robot can achieve precise footfall placement and dynamic stabilization by explicitly reasoning about and exploiting estimated terrain properties (e.g., slope, compliance) in real-time.
proposals.method: We will extend the 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance and virtual reference point concepts to the domain of legged locomotion. A tightly-coupled estimator will fuse monocular visual-inertial odometry (e.g., VINS-Mono) with contact-aided inertial navigation and a probabilistic terrain contact model. This estimated terrain-relative state will feed an adaptive guidance law that dynamically adjusts virtual target points and desired velocities based on inferred terrain roughness and slip, generating stable reference trajectories for a whole-body controller.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel guidance and control architecture for legged robots that inverts the standard reliance on precise exteroceptive mapping. 2) Theoretical stability guarantees (e.g., USGES) for the coupled estimation-guidance system in the presence of bounded visual uncertainty. 3) Experimental validation on a quadruped robot performing dynamic gaits and precision landing on unknown, rough outdoor terrain.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of ALOS-based kinematic guidance—traditionally for marine vehicles—with proprioceptive-terrain inference for legged locomotion, creating a unified framework that treats visual perception not as a mapping module but as an adaptive component of a stability-guaranteed guidance loop.

proposals.title: Adaptive Hierarchical Model Selection for Real-Time Biomechanical Parameter Estimation on Resource-Constrained Wearables
proposals.motivation: Real-time estimation of biomechanical parameters (e.g., joint angles, moments) from wearable sensors is crucial for movement analysis and assistive device control. However, existing methods typically rely on a single, fixed-complexity model, which is either too simplistic for dynamic activities or too computationally expensive for sustained use on low-power devices. This creates a fundamental trade-off between accuracy, robustness across diverse movements, and practical deployment on wearable platforms with variable computational load.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive guidance and estimation framework that dynamically selects the simplest viable model from a pre-defined hierarchy, based on real-time assessment of movement complexity and processor load, will provide robust, real-time parameter estimation across a wide activity spectrum without exceeding computational constraints.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer architecture inspired by guidance and control systems for autonomous vehicles. The lower layer consists of a hierarchy of biomechanical models (e.g., from inverted pendulum to full multi-segment dynamics) with associated Unscented Kalman Filters (UKFs) for state estimation. The upper layer is an adaptive supervisor, analogous to a path-following guidance law, that continuously monitors features of the inertial sensor data (e.g., spectral content, amplitude) and the device's CPU utilization. Using a kinematic switching logic, similar to an Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) decision rule, it selects the appropriate model from the hierarchy to minimize computational cost while maintaining estimation error below a defined threshold.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel estimation framework that guarantees real-time performance on wearable hardware by formalizing the model selection process. It will provide a principled method to extend the operational envelope of biomechanical monitoring from simple gait to complex sports, all on a single, resource-constrained device. The results will advance the field of wearable robotics and human motion analysis by solving the fidelity-latency trade-off through intelligent system-level adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While model switching exists in control theory, its application to biomechanical estimation is novel. The explicit fusion of movement complexity assessment with real-time compute resource monitoring to drive the model selection creates a new, holistic approach for wearable sensor processing, moving beyond static model choices.

proposals.title: A Differentiable Skinning Prior for Robust Articulated Motion Estimation in Marine Vehicle Manipulator Systems
proposals.motivation: Estimating the precise, articulated motion of robotic manipulators or flexible appendages on marine vehicles is critical for autonomous intervention and inspection tasks. Pre-2026, multi-sensor fusion (e.g., visual-inertial odometry) provides noisy, often inconsistent 3D coordinate streams, especially in challenging underwater or surface environments. Existing optimization frameworks lack strong, physically plausible priors that can project this noisy data onto a manifold of kinematically valid and visually realistic motions.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a differentiable linear blend skinning (LBS) model and its associated rendering pipeline directly into a robust sensor fusion optimization loop, the system can leverage visual plausibility and kinematic constraints as a soft prior, yielding more stable, accurate, and operator-interpretable estimates of complex articulated motion.
proposals.method: The framework will establish a factor-graph optimizer (e.g., GTSAM) that fuses inconsistent 3D feature points from multi-view geometry and inertial measurements. The novel factor will be a 'skinning prior' that penalizes deviations from a deformable mesh driven by an underlying kinematic skeleton. Using automatic differentiation (e.g., PyTorch), the gradient of the rendering loss—measuring the discrepancy between a synthesized view and sensor input—will be backpropagated through the skinning and bone transformation updates to correct joint states and sensor biases.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a new paradigm where computer graphics techniques actively constrain state estimation, moving visualization from a passive output to an integral part of the solver. It will provide a robust estimation framework for marine manipulators with quantifiable stability guarantees under sensor degradation, directly extending Fossen's work on guidance and kinematic control to the articulated, non-rigid domain.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in making the entire graphics skinning and rendering pipeline a differentiable component within a real-time sensor fusion optimizer, using visual appearance as a direct feedback signal to correct kinematic and geometric states, which is distinct from post-process smoothing or offline animation techniques.

proposals.title: Inverse Kinematic Motion Generation for Robotic Manipulators via Geophysical Seismic Inversion Methods
proposals.motivation: In robotics and autonomous vehicle guidance, generating feasible and optimal kinematic trajectories from high-level task specifications remains a core challenge.
