Current methods for solving this inverse kinematics problem often rely on analytical or local optimization techniques that can struggle with complex constraints, redundancy, and global optimality. A robust, iterative framework inspired by well-established inverse problem solvers from other fields could significantly enhance motion planning capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: The iterative model-update paradigm of geophysical full-waveform inversion (FWI), where a forward model is refined to match observed data, can be successfully adapted to solve the inverse kinematics problem, generating physically plausible and task-optimal motion by treating a kinematic guidance law as the forward simulator.
proposals.method: We will formulate the task as an optimization problem where a forward simulator, based on a 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) or Virtual Reference Point kinematic guidance law, predicts vehicle/manipulator motion from a parameter set (e.g., virtual target dynamics, controller gains). The discrepancy between the simulated motion outcome and the desired task goal defines a loss function. We will employ a gradient-based optimization loop, analogous to FWI, using adjoint-state methods to efficiently compute gradients of the loss with respect to the input parameters, iteratively refining them until the simulated motion satisfies the task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, optimization-based framework for inverse kinematic motion generation with guaranteed stability through the use of proven guidance laws as the forward model. It will extend the application of seismic inversion techniques to robotics, offering a principled method to handle kinematic constraints and task objectives simultaneously.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual and algorithmic translation of full-waveform inversion, a geophysical method for subsurface imaging, to the domain of kinematic guidance and motion generation for autonomous systems is a novel interdisciplinary crossover. It moves beyond traditional inverse kinematics solvers by embedding a stable guidance system within an iterative, gradient-based parameter estimation loop.

proposals.title: Inverse Kinematic Guidance for Human Motion Synthesis: A Polymatrix Game and ALOS-Based Framework for Task-Driven Prediction
proposals.motivation: In maritime vessel traffic services and human-robot collaboration, predicting human operator motion is critical for safety and efficiency. Existing models often treat human motion as an exogenous disturbance rather than a goal-directed process that can be formally modeled and inverted. This limits the ability of autonomous systems to anticipate and adapt to human actions in shared workspaces.
proposals.hypothesis: That human goal-directed motion for a specified task can be synthesized by inverting a guidance-based behavioral model, where the task parameters define a virtual target and the human's kinematic response is generated by a stable guidance law solving a local decision-making game.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem as a polymatrix game, where the human's internal cost function (modeled from data) is minimized subject to task constraints. The solution provides a time-varying virtual reference point (VRP). A 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, with proven Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) properties, will then generate the synthetic kinematic trajectory by guiding a point-mass model from an initial state to this VRP. The framework will be validated using pre-2026 human motion datasets from simulated ship bridge operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, stability-guaranteed framework for human motion synthesis rooted in guidance and control theory. It will advance decision-support systems by enabling the simulation of plausible human trajectories for given tasks, improving predictive capabilities in VTS and human-in-the-loop automation.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of human motion by inverting a guidance model is novel, as is the integration of polymatrix game theory for task parameterization with a rigorously stable ALOS guidance law for trajectory generation, moving beyond descriptive statistical models.

proposals.title: A Tightly-Coupled Guidance and Control Strategy for Independent Propulsion-Heading Optimization in Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Current guidance and control architectures for marine vehicles often treat speed (propulsion) and heading control as separate, cascaded loops, which can lead to suboptimal performance during complex maneuvers or in the presence of strong environmental disturbances. A tightly coupled approach is necessary to fully exploit a vessel's maneuvering capabilities for tasks like dynamic positioning, precise trajectory tracking in currents, or energy-efficient transit. This is particularly relevant for autonomous underwater and surface vehicles where actuator constraints and energy are critical.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a joint optimization problem for propulsion and heading commands within a unified guidance and control framework, we can achieve superior path-following performance, improved disturbance rejection, and enhanced energy efficiency compared to traditional decoupled designs.
proposals.method: The method builds upon the researcher's recent work on 3-D adaptive line-of-sight (ALOS) guidance and virtual reference point kinematics. We will develop a nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) scheme where the cost function directly trades off cross-track error, heading error, and propulsion effort. The vehicle's kinematic model, incorporating a virtual target for path-following, will be used as the prediction model. Stability will be analyzed using tools for uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) systems, extending the guarantees from the spherical ALOS formulation to the coupled control law.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, provably stable control architecture that explicitly coordinates propulsion and heading for marine vehicles. It will provide a theoretical framework for analyzing the performance-energy trade-off in coupled guidance-control systems. The results will be validated in simulations for both underwater and surface vehicle models, demonstrating tangible benefits in scenarios with currents and complex 3-D paths.
proposals.novelty: While NMPC has been applied to marine vehicles, its use for the explicit, real-time joint optimization of propulsion and heading within a virtual-target-based guidance law is novel. The key innovation is the integration of the ALOS guidance philosophy—with its stability guarantees—into a receding-horizon optimization that directly manipulates both longitudinal and rotational dynamics.

proposals.title: Unified Guidance and Control for Marine Vehicles in High-Fidelity Environmental Disturbances: A Kinematic ALOS Framework with Spectral Wave Models
proposals.motivation: Current guidance and control designs for marine vehicles often rely on simplified, time-invariant disturbance models (e.g., constant currents or first-order wave filters), which fail to capture the complex, multi-scale, and spatially varying nature of real ocean environments. This gap limits the performance and robustness guarantees of autonomous systems in challenging offshore operations. Integrating high-fidelity models of waves, wind, and currents into the control synthesis is a critical step toward certifiable autonomy in complex maritime domains.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a high-fidelity, computationally efficient spectral wave model and 3D current field into the kinematic guidance law, an Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) based control system will achieve Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) path-following with demonstrably superior performance and reduced actuator wear compared to designs using simplified disturbance models.
proposals.method: The method extends the 3D ALOS and virtual reference point kinematic guidance frameworks developed by Fossen's group. We will integrate a pre-2026 state-of-the-art environmental model: a directional wave spectrum (e.g., JONSWAP/Pierson-Moskowitz) coupled with a first-order wave-induced force model for the vehicle dynamics, and a 3D spatially varying current field. This composite disturbance model will be incorporated as a feedforward term and as a known perturbation within the Lyapunov-based stability analysis for the ALOS guidance law. The control architecture will be tested in a high-fidelity numerical simulation (e.g., using Marine Systems Simulator) with a nonlinear dynamic model of an AUV/USV.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, stability-guaranteed guidance and control design methodology explicitly accounting for high-fidelity environmental disturbances. It will deliver a benchmark simulation environment for testing marine controllers under realistic conditions. The results will advance the field toward performance-based certification of autonomous marine systems operating in dynamic oceans.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal integration of a spectral wave model—representing a colored, time- and directionally-varying disturbance—into the kinematic ALOS stability proof, moving beyond the standard assumption of constant or slowly varying biases. This creates a direct bridge between high-fidelity oceanographic models and provably stable guidance laws.

proposals.title: Energy-Optimal Ship Path Following via Transfer of Aerospace Pseudospectral Guidance Algorithms
proposals.motivation: Maritime transport faces increasing pressure to reduce fuel consumption and emissions. While path-following control for marine vehicles is well-established, most algorithms prioritize geometric convergence over energy efficiency. In contrast, aerospace guidance has a rich history of computationally efficient trajectory optimization algorithms, such as pseudospectral methods, designed for fuel-optimal maneuvers in constrained environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That pseudospectral optimal control algorithms, used for missile and aircraft trajectory planning, can be successfully adapted to generate energy-optimal guidance references for ships, and that these references can be tracked by a kinematic guidance law like 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) to achieve globally stable, fuel-efficient path following.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the Gauss Pseudospectral Method (GPM) to solve a ship-specific energy minimization problem subject to hydrodynamic constraints (e.g., turning rate, speed). The resulting optimal speed and heading profiles will serve as a time-parameterized virtual reference trajectory. This reference will be tracked using a modified 3-D ALOS guidance law, extended to follow a moving virtual target rather than a static path. Stability will be analyzed using the USGES framework from Fossen's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, two-layer guidance architecture for ships that explicitly minimizes energy consumption. It will provide a formal methodology for transferring a class of aerospace optimization algorithms to the marine domain and deliver a provably stable, implementable guidance system validated through simulation case studies.
proposals.novelty: The direct transfer and reformulation of pseudospectral optimal control for ship energy economy is novel, as is the integration of such an optimization layer with a kinematic ALOS guidance law to create a complete, stable guidance system for ships.

proposals.title: Distributed Adaptive Guidance for Micro-Surface Vehicle Swarms: A Kinematic Game-Theoretic Framework
proposals.motivation: Micro- and nano-robotic boats, operating in swarms, hold immense potential for environmental monitoring, distributed sensing, and micro-plastics remediation. However, their extreme size constraints limit onboard computation, sensing, and actuation, making centralized control and classical guidance laws infeasible. There is a critical need for ultra-lightweight, distributed guidance strategies that are provably stable and robust to the severe disturbances and scaling effects dominant at these scales.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized control architecture, combining a simplified kinematic guidance law with an evolutionary game-theoretic decision layer, can enable robust collective path-following and obstacle avoidance for micro-surface vehicle swarms, despite severe individual hardware limitations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Virtual Reference Point (VRP) kinematic guidance law to a 2D, ultra-simplified form suitable for micro-vehicle processors. This will be integrated with a distributed, pairwise polymatrix game framework, inspired by Fossen's VTS work, to manage inter-vehicle coordination and collision avoidance. Each vehicle will execute a minimal ALOS (Adaptive Line-of-Sight) algorithm for path-following, with its reference parameters being influenced by the local game-theoretic outcomes. Stability will be analyzed using input-to-state stability (ISS) and ultimate boundedness arguments for the coupled guidance-decision system.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally frugal hybrid guidance-coordination framework specifically validated for the micro-robotic domain. It will provide formal stability guarantees for swarm behaviors under scaled-down constraints, bridging the gap between high-level multi-agent systems theory and low-level robotic implementation at the micro-scale.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a provably stable, minimalist kinematic guidance law (ALOS/VRP) with a distributed evolutionary game decision process, tailored explicitly for the severe resource constraints and dominant disturbance regimes of micro- and nano-scale surface vehicles, a domain largely unexplored in Fossen's prior marine control work.

proposals.title: Integral Line-of-Sight Guidance with Speed-Dependent Sideslip Compensation for 3D Path-Following of Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Most guidance laws for autonomous marine vehicles, including the widely used Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) method, assume a constant positive forward speed. This is a significant limitation in practical operations where speed must vary due to mission requirements, energy management, or environmental disturbances. Furthermore, sideslip angles, often treated as constant or slowly varying, are inherently coupled with vehicle speed, leading to degraded path-following performance under variable speed profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the dynamic relationship between forward speed and sideslip within a kinematic guidance framework, and integrating an adaptive observer, a unified control law can be designed that guarantees uniform semiglobal exponential stability (USGES) for 3D path-following under arbitrarily varying, positive forward speed.
proposals.method: The research will extend the 3D ALOS guidance structure using a spherical amplitude-phase formulation. A key innovation will be the derivation of a speed-dependent sideslip dynamics model, integrated into the guidance law. An adaptive integral term will be incorporated to estimate and compensate for the speed-induced sideslip variations. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov theory, ensuring USGES for the cascaded guidance-and-control system. The method will be validated in numerical simulations with a 6-DOF AUV model under varying speed commands and ocean currents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, stability-guaranteed guidance framework that relaxes a fundamental assumption in marine vehicle guidance, enhancing operational flexibility and robustness. It will deliver a directly implementable algorithm for path-following under variable speed, a critical capability for energy-efficient and adaptive mission planning. The results will advance the field of marine guidance and control by formally bridging speed management and cross-track error dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While variable speed has been addressed in trajectory tracking, its integration into stability-guaranteed 3D path-following guidance with explicit, adaptive compensation for speed-dependent sideslip is novel. This moves beyond treating sideslip as a separate disturbance to formally coupling it with the speed control variable within the ALOS architecture.

proposals.title: A 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance Law for Underwater Vehicles with Explicit Compensation of Oceanic Drift
proposals.motivation: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) performing seabed surveys or pipeline inspections must follow complex 3D paths in the presence of unknown, time-varying ocean currents (drift). Existing 3D guidance laws, such as the 3D ALOS formulation, often assume negligible drift or treat it as a disturbance to be rejected by the controller, which can lead to significant cross-track error and energy inefficiency. Explicitly integrating drift compensation into the 3D guidance loop is a critical, unsolved challenge for reliable long-duration AUV operations.
proposals.hypothesis: By extending the 2D horizontal-plane guidance law with integral action for drift estimation to three dimensions and integrating it with a spherical amplitude-phase 3D ALOS framework, it is possible to achieve uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path-following for AUVs in the presence of constant or slowly varying 3D oceanic drift.
proposals.method: The method builds upon Fossen's recent spherical amplitude-phase 3D ALOS guidance structure. We will augment this kinematic guidance law with a 3D integral term to estimate and compensate for the drift vector's components in the path-tangential reference frame. The stability of the combined guidance and drift estimation system will be analyzed using Lyapunov theory to formally guarantee USGES. The proposed guidance law will be validated in a high-fidelity simulation environment using a nonlinear dynamic model of a REMUS-class AUV, following helical and waypoint-based 3D paths under realistic current disturbances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously stable, practical 3D path-following guidance law that explicitly estimates and compensates for drift, directly reducing steady-state cross-track and vertical errors. It will extend the proven 2D integral LOS methodology into the full 3D operational domain, contributing a key module for robust autonomy in underwater vehicles.
proposals.novelty: While 2D drift-compensating ALOS is established, the novel integration of a 3D drift estimator within a spherical amplitude-phase ALOS guidance framework, with formal USGES guarantees for the combined system, has not been previously demonstrated. This moves beyond treating drift as a mere disturbance to actively canceling it within the guidance loop.

proposals.title: Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance with Online Estimation of Time-Varying Sideslip Angles for Underactuated Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: In marine guidance and path-following, sideslip angles induced by ocean currents, wind, and wave drift are often assumed constant. This assumption is frequently violated in dynamic coastal or estuarine environments, leading to degraded path-following accuracy and potential instability. Extending adaptive guidance laws to handle time-varying sideslip is crucial for robust autonomy of underactuated surface and underwater vehicles in real-world, variable conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: By augmenting the existing Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) kinematic guidance law with a parameter estimator designed for time-varying parameters, such as a gradient-based adaptive law with a forgetting factor or a disturbance observer-based approach, it is possible to achieve uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path-following in the presence of slowly time-varying sideslip angles.
proposals.method: The research will extend the 3-D ALOS guidance framework from Fossen's recent work. The core technical approach is to replace the constant parameter update law with an adaptive law suitable for time-varying parameters, such as a gradient algorithm with σ-modification or a composite learning law integrating historical data. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov theory and the notion of uniform stability for non-autonomous systems. The method will be validated through high-fidelity numerical simulations of an underactuated AUV model subjected to simulated spatially and temporally varying ocean currents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, stable guidance law that explicitly compensates for time-varying sideslip, a significant step beyond current constant-parameter ALOS methods. It will deliver a directly implementable algorithm for marine vehicle autopilots, enhancing their operational robustness in complex flow fields. The theoretical analysis will contribute to the broader field of adaptive control for systems with time-varying uncertainties.
proposals.novelty: While ALOS guidance for constant sideslip is well-established, the explicit integration and stability-guaranteed compensation of *time-varying* sideslip within the kinematic guidance loop is a novel extension. The novelty lies in the tailored adaptive law design and the subsequent non-autonomous stability proof for the integrated guidance system.

proposals.title: Robust 3-D Path-Following for Vehicles with Complex Dynamics: Experimental Validation of a Virtual Reference Point Kinematic Guidance Law
proposals.motivation: Advanced guidance laws, such as the Virtual Reference Point (VRP) and 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) methods, have demonstrated strong stability guarantees for underactuated vehicles. However, their performance and robustness when applied to vehicles with highly coupled, nonlinear, and fully-actuated dynamics—common in agile UAVs and modern dynamic positioning ships—remain largely unexplored in experimental settings. Validating these controllers on such platforms is critical for transitioning from theoretical stability proofs to reliable deployment in complex operational environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The VRP kinematic guidance law, when integrated with a suitable inner-loop dynamic controller, will provide robust and uniformly globally asymptotically stable (UGAS) or uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) 3-D path-following for vehicles with complex, fully-actuated dynamics, despite significant model uncertainties and environmental disturbances.
proposals.method: The project will implement the pre-2026 VRP guidance law as the outer-loop kinematic controller. For a fully-actuated surface vessel, we will integrate it with a nonlinear dynamic control law, such as a vectorial backstepping or sliding-mode controller, to handle coupled surge-sway-yaw-heave dynamics. For a multirotor UAV, the VRP output will feed into a cascaded attitude controller. The combined guidance-control system will be tested in high-fidelity simulations (using Fossen's marine simulator models and Gazebo for UAVs) and validated experimentally on a small-scale autonomous catamaran and a hexacopter, following complex 3-D paths (e.g., lemniscates, helical climbs). Performance will be evaluated using cross-track error, heading/attitude response, and control effort metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first experimental validations of the VRP/ALOS guidance framework for complex vehicle dynamics, bridging a gap between theoretical kinematics and practical dynamics. It will yield a publicly available dataset and a tuned, modular guidance-control architecture applicable to a broader class of autonomous vehicles. The results will inform necessary modifications to the guidance law for high-performance applications.
proposals.novelty: While VRP/ALOS guidance is established for underactuated marine craft, its application and rigorous experimental testing on agile, fully-actuated systems (where kinematic assumptions are stressed) is novel. The integration and validation for both marine and aerial domains within a single theoretical framework is a non-trivial extension.

proposals.title: Disturbance Observer-Based Sideslip Estimation and Compensation for Robust 3-D Path-Following of Marine Vehicles
proposals.motivation: Marine vehicles operating in currents and waves experience significant sideslip, degrading the performance of traditional Line-of-Sight (LOS) guidance laws. While adaptive LOS (ALOS) methods exist, their parameter adaptation can be slow, leading to transient tracking errors and reduced robustness in dynamic, uncertain environments. Faster and more accurate sideslip estimation is therefore critical for high-precision autonomous operations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDOB) into a 3-D kinematic guidance law will provide fast, accurate estimation of sideslip angles, enabling near-instantaneous disturbance rejection and significantly improving the transient and steady-state path-following performance of underactuated marine vehicles.
proposals.method: The research will extend the Virtual Reference Point (VRP) or spherical ALOS kinematic guidance frameworks from Fossen's recent work. A first-order or higher-order NDOB, prevalent in robotics for force estimation, will be designed to estimate the lumped disturbance representing sideslip. This estimate will be fed directly into the guidance law's lookahead and course-angle calculations for real-time compensation. The closed-loop system's stability will be analyzed using Input-to-State Stability (ISS) or UGES/ULES frameworks, combining the kinematics with the observer dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel guidance architecture that merges the geometric strengths of 3-D LOS with the dynamic estimation capabilities of DOBs. It will deliver a theoretically-grounded, practical method for dramatically improving path-following accuracy in the presence of rapidly changing ocean currents. The result is a step towards guidance systems that are both geometrically sound and dynamically aware.
proposals.novelty: While DOBs are common in force control for robotic manipulators, their application for direct sideslip estimation and compensation within a 3-D kinematic guidance law for marine vehicles is novel. This approach moves beyond parameter adaptation to direct disturbance observation, promising faster rejection than conventional adaptive methods.

proposals.title: Model Predictive Path Following with Drift Compensation for Marine Vehicles: A Kinematic ALOS-MPC Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Accurate 3-D path following for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) and marine craft is challenged by persistent environmental disturbances, such as ocean currents, which cause drift. While Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance provides robust stability, its reactive nature lacks the ability to proactively optimize vehicle motion over a future horizon to minimize cross-track error and energy use. Integrating preview-based optimization is a critical next step for high-performance autonomy in dynamic seas.
proposals.hypothesis: Synthesizing a kinematic ALOS guidance law with a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework will yield a path-following controller that explicitly compensates for predicted drift, outperforming standard ALOS in terms of integrated cross-track error and control effort under time-varying currents.
proposals.method: The method will extend the 3-D kinematic guidance law from Fossen's recent work by embedding it as the core prediction model within a linear MPC. The MPC's cost function will penalize future cross-track and vertical errors relative to a virtual target, alongside control effort (surge, angular rates). A disturbance estimator will provide a feedforward prediction of current-induced drift over the horizon. The MPC will solve the optimization online to compute optimal velocity commands, which are then tracked by the vehicle's low-level dynamic controllers.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computationally feasible MPC guidance law for 3-D path following with formal stability guarantees inherited from the ALOS structure. It will provide a benchmark for preview-based guidance in marine robotics, demonstrating quantifiable performance gains in simulation and potentially field trials for AUVs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific synthesis of the well-established, stable ALOS kinematic model with an MPC preview optimizer for marine vehicles, creating a new hybrid guidance strategy that is not a direct application of either method alone. This addresses the explicit optimization of future guidance actions against predicted drift, a gap in pre-2026 ALOS literature.

proposals.title: Robust 3-D Path-Following for Marine Vehicles: An Adaptive Line-of-Sight Guidance and Sliding Mode Control Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Autonomous marine vehicles operating in dynamic ocean environments are subject to significant sideslip and model uncertainties, which degrade the performance of standard guidance and control laws. Pre-2026 research, including Fossen's work on 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance, provides stability guarantees but requires integration with robust control to handle these persistent disturbances effectively. A unified solution is needed to ensure precise path-following under realistic, uncertain conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a sliding mode controller (SMC) with a 3-D ALOS guidance law will yield a cascaded system with uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) path-following error dynamics, robust to bounded sideslip, current disturbances, and parametric model uncertainties.
proposals.method: The method will synthesize a cascaded guidance and control system. The guidance loop will employ the 3-D ALOS law, formulated in spherical coordinates, to generate desired attitude and velocity references. The control loop will design a continuous SMC law, potentially using higher-order or adaptive gains, to track these references. The closed-loop stability will be proven using Lyapunov theory and cascaded systems analysis, ensuring the overall system's robustness. Validation will be conducted via numerical simulation with a 6-DOF AUV model under realistic ocean disturbances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically rigorous, robust control framework for 3-D path-following of marine vehicles, explicitly handling sideslip. It will extend the ALOS guidance paradigm by integrating it with a discontinuous control technique, delivering guaranteed performance bounds. The result will be a complete, implementable guidance and control scheme with formal stability guarantees for practitioners.
proposals.novelty: While SMC and ALOS are established individually, their integrated synthesis for 3-D path-following with formal cascaded stability analysis is novel. The specific application to counteract sideslip uncertainties within the spherical ALOS guidance framework represents a significant advancement beyond current kinematic or non-robust dynamic solutions.

proposals.title: A Distributed Kinematic Guidance and Control Framework for Ship Convoy Coordination Using Adaptive Line-of-Sight
proposals.motivation: Coordinated path-following for convoys of autonomous or semi-autonomous surface vessels is critical for improving maritime logistics efficiency, safety, and fuel economy. Existing centralized solutions for multi-vessel systems face scalability and single-point-of-failure issues, while decentralized approaches often lack rigorous stability guarantees for the complete formation under environmental disturbances.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed guidance and control architecture, built upon the proven Uniformly Semiglobally Exponentially Stable (USGES) 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) guidance law, can guarantee stable and precise formation path-following for a convoy of ships under model uncertainties and ocean currents.
proposals.method: The core method extends the virtual reference point kinematic guidance law to a leader-follower convoy structure. Each vessel employs its own 3-D ALOS guidance law to follow a path, but the path parameter for followers is dynamically coupled to the leader's state to maintain formation. A distributed consensus protocol will adjust the virtual target points, ensuring cohesive convoy behavior. The overall architecture will be validated through Lyapunov-based stability analysis and high-fidelity simulations using models of marine surface vessels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, provably stable distributed guidance framework for ship convoys, explicitly extending the single-vehicle ALOS theory to coordinated fleets. It will provide a practical and scalable solution for maritime formation control, backed by rigorous stability guarantees, filling a gap between single-agent path-following and multi-agent coordination.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the robust, adaptive single-vehicle ALOS guidance—a hallmark of Fossen's recent work—with a distributed coordination layer to create a unified convoy control system with formal stability proofs, which is not a straightforward extension of existing single-agent results.

proposals.title: Dual-Use Path-Following Control: Simultaneous Guidance and Ocean Current Profiling via Sideslip Estimation
proposals.motivation: Accurate, real-time estimation of ocean currents is critical for autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) navigation, energy efficiency, and environmental monitoring. Traditional methods rely on dedicated sensors (e.g., ADCPs) or model-based observers, which add cost, complexity, or require extensive hydrodynamic models. A method to infer currents directly from the guidance and control system would enable persistent, sensor-less environmental sensing as a byproduct of normal vehicle operation.
proposals.hypothesis: The sideslip angle (or velocity) estimated by a 3-D adaptive line-of-sight (ALOS) or virtual reference point guidance law, designed for path-following in the presence of unknown currents, provides a stable and accurate estimate of the horizontal cross-track current component, enabling the vehicle to act as a moving current profiler.
proposals.method: We will extend the 3-D ALOS guidance law with uniformly semiglobally exponentially stable (USGES) guarantees or the virtual reference point kinematic guidance law, both previously developed by Fossen's group. The core innovation is to formally treat the adaptive sideslip estimate not just as a control parameter but as a direct measurement of the current-induced drift. A cascaded observer architecture will be designed: the inner loop performs standard path-following, while an outer, slower update filter integrates the time- and space-varying sideslip estimates along the vehicle's trajectory to reconstruct a 2D current vector field. Stability analysis will ensure the dual-use does not degrade guidance performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for dual-use guidance systems that perform simultaneous path-following and current field mapping. It contributes a method for sensor-less current estimation, reducing AUV dependency on physical current sensors. The result is a validated control-theoretic approach to turn the AUV itself into an adaptive oceanographic sensor.
proposals.novelty: While sideslip estimation is used to *reject* currents for control, its direct repurposing as a quantitative, profiling current *sensor* within a stable guidance loop is novel. This creates a symbiotic dual function where control informs sensing and sensing enhances situational awareness for navigation, moving beyond treating disturbances purely as a nuisance.

proposals.title: Anticipatory Underwater Formation Control: A Model Predictive Control Framework for UUV Leader-Follower Systems with Kinematic Guidance
proposals.motivation: Leader-follower control for Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUVs) is critical for collaborative oceanographic surveys and subsea infrastructure inspection. Traditional Line-of-Sight (LOS) guidance laws, while robust, are reactive and can struggle with the anticipatory path-tracking required for tight formation keeping in dynamic currents and around complex 3D paths. Model Predictive Control (MPC), widely successful in aerial swarm robotics for its predictive and constraint-handling capabilities, offers a promising but underexplored solution for underwater cooperative autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: A Model Predictive Control framework, integrating a kinematic guidance law as a virtual reference generator, will provide superior anticipatory tracking and formation-keeping performance for a UUV follower compared to standard reactive LOS-based controllers, especially in the presence of ocean currents and complex leader maneuvers.
proposals.method: The core method is a hierarchical MPC architecture. The outer loop uses a 3-D Adaptive LOS (ALOS) or Virtual Reference Point (VRP) kinematic guidance law to generate a smooth, feasible reference trajectory for the follower, accounting for the leader's predicted path. This reference is fed into a constrained, finite-horizon nonlinear MPC inner loop. The MPC will solve an online optimization problem to compute optimal control inputs, explicitly incorporating the UUV's dynamic model, actuator constraints, and predicted ocean current disturbances. Stability will be analyzed using Input-to-State Stability (ISS) or USGES frameworks for the cascaded system.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practically implementable MPC framework for UUV leader-follower systems, validated in simulation with high-fidelity models. It will provide a formal stability analysis for the integrated guidance-and-control structure and demonstrate quantifiable performance gains in anticipatory tracking and disturbance rejection over conventional methods, advancing the state of cooperative underwater vehicle control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled transfer and adaptation of MPC-based *formation* control paradigms from aerial swarms to the underwater *leader-follower* problem, coupled with the explicit integration of Fossen-group kinematic guidance laws (ALOS/VRP) as the reference generator within the MPC prediction horizon, creating a cohesive anticipatory control system.

proposals.title: Adaptive Leader Guidance for Heterogeneous Vehicle Formations: An Inverse Control Paradigm with Stability Guarantees
proposals.motivation: In multi-vehicle systems, conventional leader-follower control places the burden of tracking on followers, often leading to failure if the leader's trajectory exceeds follower capabilities (e.g., dynamics, actuator limits). This is critical for heterogeneous marine or underwater vehicle teams where vehicles have differing performance envelopes. A paradigm where the leader adapts its path in real-time can enhance overall mission robustness and success.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the control paradigm and formulating the leader's guidance as an adaptive function of the estimated follower state and performance envelope, the overall formation system can achieve stable path-following for a broader class of trajectories and under greater model uncertainty.
proposals.method: The method integrates a kinematic guidance law for the leader, such as a 3-D Adaptive Line-of-Sight (ALOS) or Virtual Reference Point law, but with its reference path or speed modulated by a real-time estimate of the follower's tracking error and capability. A Lyapunov-based stability analysis will be conducted, extending the USGES (Uniform Semiglobal Exponential Stability) framework from Fossen's recent ALOS work to the coupled leader-follower system. The follower's capability will be estimated via an observer using its tracking error and known dynamic constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, stable control architecture for leader-adaptive formations, expanding the theory of guidance systems. It will provide a practical solution for operating heterogeneous autonomous vehicle teams in challenging environments, increasing mission reliability. The formal stability guarantees will bridge the gap between ad-hoc adaptive strategies and rigorous nonlinear control.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on follower adaptation, the core novelty is the systematic inversion of the control problem to make the leader's guidance law explicitly dependent on the follower's real-time state and performance limits, with formal stability proofs. This moves beyond traditional hierarchical structures towards a more cooperative, capability-aware framework.

ideas.title: Robust Proprioceptive Navigation from Imperfect Visual Observations
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms for legged robots or drones to perform precise landings and dynamic locomotion on completely unknown, unprepared terrain. It inverts the reliance on high-quality exteroceptive data by fusing noisy, monocular, or markerless video streams with robust proprioceptive sensing and contact models. The core synergy is a unified state estimation and planning framework that treats visual uncertainty and terrain geometry as a single, adaptive inference problem, enabling the system to reason about and exploit terrain properties (like compliance or roughness) in real-time for stabilization without prior maps. This enables autonomous operation in natural, GPS-denied environments using only commodity cameras and onboard sensors.

ideas.title: Inverse Kinematics via Geophysical Seismic Inversion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem in geophysical seismic inversion to the domain of human motion analysis. In seismology, one infers unknown subsurface properties from surface wave measurements by iteratively running forward simulations and updating the model. Here, the analogous challenge is to generate (infer) the precise motion kinematics from a set of high-level task parameters and biomechanical constraints. The adapted research idea proposes developing an optimization framework where a forward human motion simulator (the estimated model) is used inside a loop. Starting from an initial guess of parameters (like strength, coordination patterns, or objective weights), the simulator predicts motion. This prediction is compared to a target motion defined by the task (e.g., 'reach point X,' 'lift weight Y'). A loss function quantifies the difference, and its gradient with respect to the input parameters is used to adjust them, iteratively refining the parameters until the simulator outputs a motion that successfully completes the specified task. This provides a principled, optimization-based method for the inverse problem of motion generation from parameters.

ideas.title: Biomechanics-Adaptive HD Lidar for Dynamic Terrain and Morphology Mapping
ideas.description: This research fuses next-generation HD Lidar development with non-human biomechanics parameter estimation to create an adaptive, perception-for-modeling sensor system. The core innovation is a Lidar that uses its high-resolution 3D data stream to simultaneously map the external terrain and estimate the dynamic internal state (e.g., joint angles, forces, gait parameters) of moving animals or robots within that environment. By closing the loop, the sensor's own scanning pattern, resolution, and frame rate can be optimized in real-time to reduce uncertainty in the estimated biomechanical model, providing a denser and more intelligent data feed. This enables unprecedented capabilities for studying animal locomotion in the wild and for robotic systems that must dynamically interact with complex, moving obstacles.

ideas.title: Real-time, adaptive model selection for robust parameter estimation across diverse movements and compute constraints
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that validates biomechanical parameter estimation not on a single model, but on an adaptive hierarchy of models designed for different movement complexities and computational costs. The system continuously assesses the incoming movement data (e.g., from inertial sensors) and the current load on the local processor (e.g., a wearable or mobile device) to select the simplest model that provides sufficient accuracy for the current motion. This enables real-time, robust estimation across a diverse population and activity set—from slow walking to complex sports maneuvers—on devices with limited and variable onboard compute, by intelligently trading off fidelity for latency only when necessary.

ideas.title: Visual-Inertial Skinning for Robust Articulated Motion Estimation
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that tightly couples robust optimization for inconsistent, redundant 3D coordinates (e.g., from multi-view reconstruction or IMU sensors) with real-time computer graphics skinning techniques. The core innovation is to use the visual plausibility and kinematic constraints inherent in the skinning and rendering pipeline as a soft, differentiable prior within the optimization loop. This allows the system to project noisy or conflicting estimates onto a manifold of visually and physically plausible articulated motions, which can be monitored and interactively adjusted by a human operator. The result is a more stable and interpretable estimation process for complex non-rigid motions, where visualization becomes an integral part of the solver rather than a passive endpoint.

ideas.title: Singularity-Robust Scene Understanding via Constraint-Aware Passive Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This research develops a novel perception framework for autonomous systems that forgoes dedicated Lidar by fusing data from passive sensors like cameras and radars. Its core innovation is a unified geometric and dynamic model for the environment and detected objects that is formulated from the outset to avoid representational singularities, such as those in Euler angles or other parameterizations. This singularity-avoiding formulation ensures stable, continuous state estimation and tracking even with the noisier, less direct 3D data from passive sensors. Consequently, the system achieves robust hazard detection and scene understanding without partitioning constraints or handling special cases at singularities, closing a critical reliability gap in cost-effective, vision-centric autonomy.

ideas.title: High-Definition Limb-Specific Hazard Detection and Biomechanical Estimation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a next-generation Hazard Detection Lidar sensor with enhanced resolution and frame rate specifically tailored for the detailed, non-contact observation of human limb and joint motion. The system applies a scaled-down estimation framework to this high-definition point cloud data, extracting detailed biomechanical parameters (e.g., joint angles, velocities, torques) for individual limbs in real-time. This enables the prediction of a person's immediate physical trajectory and posture with unprecedented accuracy. Consequently, the system can proactively identify and mitigate collision hazards or ergonomic risks by anticipating the interaction between the precisely tracked human kinematics and the dynamically mapped environment.

ideas.title: Residual-Driven Terrain Discovery for Adaptive Legged Landing
ideas.description: This research develops algorithms for legged robots to land precisely on completely unknown terrain by fusing real-time terrain discovery with model self-diagnosis. The core innovation is to treat persistent estimation residuals—discrepancies between predicted and actual motion—not just as noise, but as active signals indicating either unmodeled terrain properties (e.g., compliance, granularity) or inadequacies in the robot's own biomechanical model. The system uses these residuals to guide targeted exploratory motions (like a probing step or controlled impact) to disambiguate the cause and update both its terrain understanding and its self-model. This closed-loop of residual-driven probing and concurrent model adaptation allows for dynamically safe landing strategies on complex, a priori unmapped surfaces.

ideas.title: On-Device Motion Synthesis via Edge-Assisted Inverse Model Refinement
ideas.description: This research fuses real-time constraints with motion generation by developing a system where a computationally limited onboard device (e.g., AR glasses, robot, wearable) runs an ultra-efficient, simplified inverse model to synthesize motion from high-level task parameters. The key synergy is that the edge server is not used for direct, latency-sensitive processing, but instead performs periodic, compute-intensive optimization to personalize and refine the device's lightweight model based on observed user data and task outcomes. This creates a closed-loop where the device gains the capability for real-time, adaptive motion prediction tailored to the individual user, while the edge continuously improves the model's accuracy and efficiency without burdening the local processor.

ideas.title: Apply Recursive Bayesian Estimation from Geophysics for Real-Time System Identification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a recursive Bayesian estimation approach from geophysics, where methods like the Ensemble Kalman Filter are used for real-time updating of subsurface models from sequential seismic data, to the domain of dynamic system identification. The analogous geophysical solution continuously assimilates new measurements to track evolving earth properties. Adapted to engineering, the research will develop a framework for using state estimation filters (e.g., Unscented Kalman Filters) to perform online parameter tracking of complex systems. This moves beyond batch optimization, enabling real-time adaptation of model parameters and their uncertainties from streaming sensor data, thus creating an adaptive digital twin for control, prognostics, and health management.

ideas.title: Real-Time Deformable Terrain Visualization for Adaptive Landing in Unknown Environments
ideas.description: This research develops a system for autonomous vehicles to land on unknown, potentially deformable terrain without prior maps. It fuses real-time 3D reconstruction and physics-based terrain estimation with computer graphics skinning and deformation techniques to create a dynamic, visualizable terrain model. The key innovation is using the graphics pipeline not merely for display, but as an integral part of the perception-action loop: predicted ground deformation under simulated landing loads is visually rendered and fed back to refine the vehicle's control and final touchdown strategy. This enables the vehicle to 'pre-visualize' the landing consequence, adjusting in real-time to avoid hazardous sinkage or instability on surfaces like sand, snow, or loose soil.

ideas.title: Continuous Flight-Informed Model Verification for Hazard Detection Robustness
ideas.description: This approach establishes a closed-loop framework where a high-fidelity digital twin of the HD system is the core verification asset. Each flight test is designed to probe specific, high-risk corners of the operational envelope (e.g., specific lighting, terrain, and vehicle dynamics combinations) to gather calibration and challenge data. This data is used to continuously update and validate the simulation model's predictive accuracy. System performance and robustness are then verified by running millions of Monte Carlo simulations in the validated digital environment, exposing failure modes and performance boundaries far beyond what limited flight tests could directly demonstrate.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Model Kalman Filtering with Innovation Monitoring for Robust Online Parameter Tracking
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the fragility of a single-model Kalman filter by maintaining a bank of parallel state estimators, each representing a plausible model hypothesis or linearization. A supervisory layer continuously monitors the innovation sequences of all filters to compute model probabilities, dynamically reweighting or switching the combined estimate. This allows for real-time adaptation to structural changes, parameter jumps, and unmodeled nonlinearities without requiring batch reprocessing. The result is a robust online tracking framework that retains the computational efficiency of recursive filtering while being resilient to the model mismatches that cause standard approaches to fail.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed, Degradation-Aware Sensor Fusion for Landing in Obscured and Unstructured Environments
ideas.description: This research develops a robust sensor fusion framework for autonomous landing that moves beyond direct adaptation of automotive methods. It centrally addresses the critical failure mode of sensor degradation and deception in extreme off-world environments like dust plumes and shadowed, rugged terrain. The core innovation is a physics-based meta-layer that dynamically models expected sensor performance (e.g., lidar backscatter, radar clutter) to weight data streams, fused with a terrain-relative navigation thread. This ensures the system maintains a probabilistically sound state estimate even when individual sensors are temporarily blinded or providing misleading data, enabling reliable landing in conditions where standard fusion would fail.

ideas.title: Micro-Terrain SLAM for Biomechanical Parameter Estimation
ideas.description: This research applies and adapts computer vision SLAM techniques not to map large-scale landing hazards, but to reconstruct and model the micro-scale terrain deformation and interaction dynamics in real-time during contact with an isolated limb or joint. By treating the local soil, regolith, or material as a high-detail, deformable map, the system can simultaneously localize the limb and estimate the evolving terrain parameters. This enables the direct, in-situ inference of crucial biomechanical or robotic parameters—such as joint torque, tissue or material stiffness, and energy dissipation—from the observed terrain response, providing a new method for adaptive control or physiological assessment in unstructured environments.

ideas.title: Modular, Multi-Scale Hazard Detection for Robotic Mobility and Manipulation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a modular hazard detection (HD) system whose hardware suite and algorithms can be reconfigured on-the-fly to operate at vastly different spatial scales. For planetary landing and rover navigation, it functions in its traditional role, identifying large-scale terrain hazards. Upon reaching a target, the system can then scale down its sensing and computational focus to a specific robotic limb or end-effector, enabling detailed estimation of contact parameters, surface compliance, and micro-topography for precise sample acquisition or instrument placement. This dual-scale capability allows a single, adaptable autonomy core to manage both the macro-hazards of mobility and the micro-hazards of manipulation, significantly enhancing mission versatility without a proportional increase in mass or complexity.

ideas.title: Robust Motion Synthesis via Constraint-Aware Inverse Dynamics Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel inverse dynamics and control framework for human motion generation. It integrates the task of synthesizing motion from high-level parameters (like desired foot placement or object manipulation goals) with advanced optimization techniques specifically designed to handle the inherent inconsistencies and redundancies in coordinate representations of the human body. The core innovation is a generative model whose internal solver employs adaptive relaxation and projection methods to dynamically resolve kinematic and dynamic infeasibilities during motion prediction, ensuring physical plausibility. This results in a system capable of producing robust, stable motion simulations for complex tasks directly from imperfect or incomplete specifications, bridging the gap between high-level intent and low-level biomechanical realism.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Hierarchical Estimation for Joint-Specific Parameters
ideas.description: This approach estimates high-detail parameters for a target joint or limb while explicitly accounting for its dynamic coupling to the rest of the body. A two-tiered model is used: a high-fidelity model of the target region and a simplified, dynamically consistent 'anchor' model of the full body. The framework jointly optimizes parameters by ensuring forces and kinematics are compatible across the hierarchical interface, preventing physiologically invalid solutions. This yields limb-specific parameters that are accurate in isolation and remain valid when integrated into predictions of whole-body movement.

ideas.title: Morphology-Aware Meta-Framework for Parameter Estimation Across Diverse Non-Human Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a hierarchical, morphology-aware meta-framework for robust parameter estimation in animal and robotic systems. The framework first classifies the target system's fundamental mechanical structure and locomotion modality using a set of topological and dynamic primitives. Based on this classification, it deploys a specialized estimation pipeline that incorporates appropriate physical constraints, contact models, and sensor fusion strategies validated for that morphological class. This approach ensures methodological rigor across diverse systems while avoiding the pitfalls of an overly generic one-size-fits-all model.

ideas.title: Validate HD system performance using phased flight tests with surrogate targets
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes a method for validating the Hazard Detection (HD) system prototype by drawing an analogy from sonar testing in marine science. The analogous solution involves phased testing in increasingly realistic environments using surrogate targets with known properties. Adapted to this context, the research would design a flight test campaign where surrogate hazards (artificial rocks, craters) are placed in a test area. The HD system, mounted on a relevant flight platform, would then be tasked with detecting and characterizing these hazards during a series of flights, progressing from simple, stable overflights to complex, dynamic descent profiles. Performance is quantified by comparing the system's outputs against the precise ground-truth data of the surrogate targets, providing rigorous validation in flight-relevant conditions before operational deployment.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Modal Hazard Mapping via Physics-Informed Sensor Fusion for Planetary Descent
ideas.description: This system overcomes the fragility of vision-only SLAM by fusing it with complementary, physics-grounded data streams in a resilient architecture. It uses inertial measurements and orbital basemaps to provide a stable navigation backbone, while selectively incorporating visual SLAM and active depth data only when their quality metrics exceed reliability thresholds. A unified estimator continuously validates all inputs against known physical constraints of the descent trajectory, isolating and suppressing erroneous data from any single sensor. This results in a hazard map that is continuously available and reliable, even during dynamic visual obscuration events like dust plumes or shadow transitions.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Human Motion Inference from Unconstrained Video
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that infers accurate, full-body motion from low-quality video by strictly enforcing physical plausibility and biomechanical validity. The core innovation is a hybrid model that combines a deep neural network for initial pose estimation with a differentiable physics simulator and anatomical constraint modules that refine the motion. The system is trained using a loss function that penalizes kinematic errors against any available ground truth while heavily penalizing physical impossibilities, ensuring outputs are actionable for downstream tasks. This approach guarantees that even with extremely noisy or occluded input, the estimated motion remains within the feasible space of human movement.

ideas.title: Miniaturize for micro-AUV docking and inspection
ideas.description: Scale down the optical guidance system for centimeter-scale micro-AUVs performing close-range underwater structure inspection and docking.
ideas.sourceTitle: Guidance and control of ocean vehicles

ideas.title: Applying Fault-Tolerant Control Theory to Estimate and Model Compensatory Movement Strategies
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to analyze pathological or prosthetic-assisted movement by drawing an analogy from Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC) in engineering systems. In FTC, algorithms are designed to detect component failures, estimate the system's new operational parameters, and maintain functionality. Adapting this solution, we treat an injury or prosthesis as an alteration to the human motor control system's 'plant dynamics.' The core idea is to use the movement estimation method not merely for kinematic tracking, but to actively infer and model the compensatory control strategy—the neural 'reconfiguration'—implemented to accomplish tasks. By decomposing observed movements into intended and compensatory elements, this approach could quantify adaptive motor learning, assess rehabilitation efficacy, and inform the design of prosthetics that better integrate with the user's residual control schemes.

ideas.title: Terrain Reconstruction via Active Tactile Probing for Landing
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of available prior map data by developing algorithms for completely unknown terrain, drawing an analogy from robotic tactile sensing. In that field, manipulators use active tactile probing—making gentle, exploratory contact—to build a local 3D model and infer material properties of an unknown object without prior visual data. Adapted to the landing context, the solution involves equipping a lander with a short-range, active probing system (e.g., a physical rod, laser array, or impulse transmitter) that 'taps' or scans the immediate landing zone during the final descent phase. The real-time feedback from these micro-interactions is processed by algorithms to reconstruct a local, high-resolution model of contact hazards, slope, and surface strength, enabling precise and safe touchdown on terrain for which no prior high-resolution map exists.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Model Evolution via Residual-Driven Motion Synthesis
ideas.description: This fused approach establishes a closed-loop system that iteratively refines a biomechanical model. First, motion capture data is used to estimate model parameters and identify residuals that indicate structural inadequacies, such as missing degrees of freedom or force elements. These residuals are then used to formulate and solve a targeted inverse problem, generating novel, physically plausible motion trajectories specifically designed to stress-test the suspected model weaknesses. By simulating this synthesized motion with the current model and comparing it to a higher-fidelity reference (e.g., detailed physics or new experimental data), the system can validate diagnoses and automatically propose structural expansions, closing the loop for continuous model evolution.

ideas.title: In-Flight Validation of a Miniaturized HD System on a Dedicated Small Lander Testbed
ideas.description: This research direction develops a miniaturized Hazard Detection (HD) system prototype and integrates it onto a dedicated small satellite or micro-lander platform designed as a flight testbed. The core objective is to conduct in-flight validation of the scaled system's end-to-end performance, including real-time sensor data processing, terrain-relative navigation, and hazard map generation, under dynamic conditions that cannot be fully replicated on the ground. This approach directly de-risks the technology for future CubeSat-class planetary landers by proving functionality within strict mass, power, and volume constraints in the actual flight environment. The resulting testbed and dataset provide a critical bridge between laboratory prototypes and operational use on cost-constrained, high-risk exploration missions to the Moon, Mars, or small bodies.

ideas.title: Comparative Neuromechanics: A Unified Framework for Estimating Parameters in Complex, Active Muscle-Joint Systems Across Species and Robots
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified computational framework for estimating parameters in biomechanical models that incorporate advanced active muscle dynamics and detailed, non-ideal joint mechanics. The core innovation is to apply and validate this framework across a wide spectrum of non-human systems, from animals with specialized locomotion (e.g., insects, birds, cephalopods) to bio-inspired robots with compliant actuators. By confronting the complex model with the vast morphological and behavioral data from nature and engineering, the method can identify which neuromuscular parameters and control strategies are generalizable versus specialized. This cross-domain validation will ultimately yield more robust, predictive models of embodied intelligence and inform the design of next-generation adaptive robots.

ideas.title: Biomechanically-Informed Terrain Segmentation for Adaptive Locomotion Prediction
ideas.description: This research fuses medical image segmentation techniques with advanced active muscle and joint biomechanical models to create a novel framework for robotic or prosthetic locomotion. High-resolution Lidar or visual terrain data is segmented using algorithms adapted from medical imaging, but crucially, the classification labels and training data are generated dynamically by simulating how the detailed biomechanical model interacts with and responds to different terrain types (e.g., mud, gravel, slopes). The resulting terrain map is not just visual but encoded with predicted biomechanical costs (e.g., joint torque, muscle activation, stability risk). This map then feeds back into the biomechanical model in real-time, allowing it to pre-adapt its control strategies, predict stability boundaries, and optimize gait parameters for the specific terrain ahead, enabling unprecedented predictive mobility in unstructured environments.

ideas.title: Terrain Interrogation for Certified Landing on Unknown, Uncertified Surfaces
ideas.description: This approach addresses the fundamental uncertainty of purely vision-based terrain assessment by incorporating direct physical interrogation of the landing site. During the final descent, the lander uses active probes to measure subsurface density, compaction, and load-bearing capacity in real-time, creating a certified 'footprint' for landing. This allows the system to make a final go/no-go decision or perform a last-minute micro-adjustment to place legs on validated ground. The result is a robust, closed-loop landing system that guarantees safety on truly unknown terrain without prior maps by verifying the terrain's physical properties, not just its appearance.

ideas.title: Validate parameter estimation using a benchmark suite of canonical movement primitives
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes adapting the validation methodology from Computational Fluid Dynamics to create a more systematic test for human movement parameter estimation. In CFD, new models are validated against a canonical suite of benchmark flows with known solutions. Analogously, we will construct a benchmark suite of fundamental human movement primitives (like targeted reach, sit-to-stand, and gait cycles), capturing data from a diverse subject pool using high-fidelity instrumentation to establish 'ground truth'. The parameter estimation framework will be executed on this entire suite. This approach moves beyond testing on a wider but unstructured range of motions to a systematic validation that identifies specific motion types, subject characteristics, or dynamic conditions where the framework's accuracy breaks down, providing clear directions for algorithmic improvement.

ideas.title: Singularity-Aware Adaptive Model Switching via Continuity-Preserving Transitions
ideas.description: This research develops a meta-modeling framework where multiple complementary formulations of a physical system coexist. A supervisory module, using geometric or topological criteria, monitors state variables to predict the approach of a region where the active formulation becomes singular. Before the singularity is reached, the system smoothly transitions the state representation and dynamics to an alternative, non-singular formulation via a learned diffeomorphic mapping, ensuring continuity and differentiability of the integrated solution path. The core advance is the automated, mathematically consistent switching mechanism that makes singularities operationally irrelevant without claiming to eliminate them from the physics.

ideas.title: Extend to full 6-DOF pose estimation
ideas.description: Add roll estimation to the current 5-DOF optical pose detection framework for complete orientation recovery.
ideas.sourceTitle: Guidance and control of ocean vehicles

ideas.title: Adapting Adaptive Optics from Astronomy for Dynamic Atmospheric Compensation in HD Lidar
ideas.description: This research seed expands the development of a next-generation HD Lidar sensor by drawing an analogy from ground-based astronomy's solution to atmospheric distortion. In astronomy, adaptive optics systems solve the problem of atmospheric 'seeing' by using a wavefront sensor to detect distortion and a deformable mirror to correct it in real-time, dramatically improving image resolution. We adapt this solution to the HD Lidar context by proposing the integration of a co-aligned wavefront sensing channel and a high-bandwidth deformable optical element into the Lidar's transmitter. This system would dynamically measure and pre-correct the laser beam for turbulence and aerosol scattering before it leaves the sensor, analogous to how telescopes correct incoming starlight. The adapted research idea focuses on developing this closed-loop, atmospheric-compensation subsystem to maintain a stable, high-fidelity beam under variable environmental conditions, thereby significantly enhancing the Lidar's effective range, point cloud density, and hazard detection reliability for autonomous systems operating in challenging real-world atmospheres.

ideas.title: Real-time adaptive biomechanical state estimation using dual Kalman filters for complex neuromuscular models
ideas.description: This research fuses real-time Kalman filtering with complex biomechanical models to create a novel framework for adaptive state estimation. A dual or joint Kalman filter architecture is employed to simultaneously estimate dynamic neuromuscular control signals (like muscle activations and tendon forces) and update personalized model parameters (like muscle-tendon properties and joint stiffness) in real-time. This allows a high-fidelity model incorporating active muscle dynamics and detailed joint mechanics to continuously adapt to an individual's current physiological state during movement. The synergy enables unprecedented online predictive capability for applications in neurorehabilitation, prosthetic control, and sports science, where both immediate movement intent and underlying biomechanical properties are co-evolving.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Inversion Methods for Biomechanical Parameter Estimation
ideas.description: This research adapts solution frameworks from geophysical inversion to the problem of estimating parameters in non-human biomechanical or robotic system models. In geophysics, practitioners estimate unknown subsurface properties from indirect, sparse, and noisy surface measurements by employing regularization and hierarchical Bayesian methods that incorporate strong prior knowledge and physical constraints to yield stable, uncertainty-aware solutions. The adapted idea applies this approach to animal or robotic biomechanics: treating sparse kinematic/force data as the 'surface measurements,' the hard-to-measure internal biomechanical parameters (e.g., passive tissue properties, actuator dynamics) as the 'subsurface properties,' and using anatomical scaling laws and physical principles as the stabilizing priors. This would create a robust framework for parameter estimation that explicitly handles data sparsity, noise, and model redundancy while providing quantified uncertainty, directly addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension by leveraging scaling laws as informative priors within the inversion process.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion for High-Fidelity, Isolated Neuromuscular Biomechanics
ideas.description: This research adapts sensor fusion architectures from autonomous vehicles (combining Lidar for precise 3D geometry, radar for velocity through clothing, and high-speed optics for texture and markers) to the specific problem of estimating high-detail biomechanical parameters for isolated joints or limbs. The core innovation is scaling the fusion framework down from a vehicle-scale perception problem to a millimeter-scale biomechanical estimation problem, creating a portable system that operates outside the lab. This enables the continuous, precise monitoring of parameters like patellar tracking, wrist tendon displacement, or ankle joint forces during complex, unconstrained activities, providing a novel tool for sports science, rehabilitation diagnostics, and prosthetic control.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion for Robust Perception in Obscured Environments
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the nominal operational concept—often reliant on unimodal visual sensing—by proposing a solution adapted from biomedical imaging. In that field, diagnostic robustness is achieved by fusing complementary data from MRI, CT, and PET scans to overcome the limitations of any single modality. Adapted to robotics and autonomous systems, the idea is to develop and test a multi-modal sensor fusion framework for operation in dynamic or obscurant-filled environments (e.g., dust, fog, moving shadows). The research would focus on creating perception algorithms that intelligently combine inputs from LiDAR (for 3D structure), radar (for penetration through obscurants), thermal imaging (for heat signatures unaffected by visible light conditions), and event cameras (for high-dynamic-range motion data). The goal is to generate a resilient environmental model that maintains operational capability when traditional vision-based systems fail, directly testing and challenging the assumption that a single primary sensor type is sufficient for complex, real-world environments.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hybrid Estimation for Non-Standard Neuromechanical Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a novel adaptive framework for movement analysis specifically designed for the unstable dynamics of pathological or assisted movement. It combines a generative simulator, which models a wide range of possible impaired biomechanics and prosthetic interactions, with a fast-adapting meta-estimation layer. This hybrid approach allows the system to first learn a prior over possible failure modes and then quickly personalize its estimation model to an individual's unique neuromechanical system, whether affected by injury or augmented by an assistive device. The result is a robust analysis tool that does not rely on normative data assumptions and can provide accurate estimates in highly variable, non-standard movement conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Computational Fluid Dynamics Adjoint Methods for Neural Network Sensitivity Analysis
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using automatic differentiation (AD) for efficient derivative computation. Drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), where the adjoint method is used to compute sensitivities of complex simulation outputs (e.g., aerodynamic forces) with respect to a vast number of design parameters by solving a single backward equation through the simulation's computational graph. This research adapts that solution by applying the adjoint method's core principle—efficient, one-pass backward sensitivity propagation—to the analysis of deep neural networks. Using modern AD frameworks, we can formulate a 'neural adjoint' approach to compute exact derivatives of any scalar network output (like loss or a specific prediction) with respect to all parameters, layers, or inputs simultaneously. This provides a powerful, exact alternative to perturbation-based sensitivity analysis, enabling deeper model interpretability, robust feature attribution, and insights into parameter influences during and after training.
